<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the Superposition Theorem. Mention one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**
The Superposition Theorem states that in any linear bilateral network containing multiple independent sources, the response (voltage or current) in any element is the **algebraic sum** of the responses produced by each independent source acting **alone**, with all other independent sources deactivated (voltage sources replaced by short circuits, current sources by open circuits).

**Limitation:** It is **not applicable to non-linear circuits** (e.g., circuits with diodes, transistors, saturable cores) and **cannot be used to compute power** directly because $P = I^2 R$ is a non-linear (quadratic) function.

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What happens to a dependent source when applying the Superposition Theorem?**

**Model Answer:**
A **dependent source is NEVER deactivated** during the application of the Superposition Theorem. It must be **retained in the circuit** in its original form throughout all stages of analysis, because its value depends on another variable in the circuit (such as a controlling voltage or current). If a dependent source is incorrectly replaced by a short or open circuit, the controlling variable will become zero, leading to an erroneous result.

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Using the **Superposition Theorem**, find the current through the $4\ \Omega$ resistor in the circuit shown below, where $V_1 = 12\text{ V}$, $V_2 = 6\text{ V}$, $R_1 = 2\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 3\ \Omega$, and the $4\ \Omega$ resistor is connected between the junction of $R_1, R_2$ and the common ground.

**Model Solution:**

**Stage 1 — Activate $V_1$ only (Short $V_2$):**

The $3\ \Omega$ resistor is now in parallel with the $4\ \Omega$ resistor:

$$R_p = \frac{3 \times 4}{3 + 4} = \frac{12}{7}\ \Omega$$

$$R_{total,1} = R_1 + R_p = 2 + \frac{12}{7} = \frac{26}{7}\ \Omega$$

$$I_1 = \frac{V_1}{R_{total,1}} = \frac{12}{26/7} = \frac{84}{26} = \frac{42}{13}\text{ A}$$

Using current divider, current through $4\ \Omega$:

$$I_{4,1} = I_1 \times \frac{3}{3 + 4} = \frac{42}{13} \times \frac{3}{7} = \frac{18}{13}\text{ A}$$

**Stage 2 — Activate $V_2$ only (Short $V_1$):**

The $2\ \Omega$ resistor is in parallel with $4\ \Omega$:

$$R_p' = \frac{2 \times 4}{2 + 4} = \frac{8}{6} = \frac{4}{3}\ \Omega$$

$$R_{total,2} = R_2 + R_p' = 3 + \frac{4}{3} = \frac{13}{3}\ \Omega$$

$$I_2 = \frac{V_2}{R_{total,2}} = \frac{6}{13/3} = \frac{18}{13}\text{ A}$$

Current through $4\ \Omega$ (note: direction is **opposite** to Stage 1):

$$I_{4,2} = I_2 \times \frac{2}{2 + 4} = \frac{18}{13} \times \frac{2}{6} = \frac{6}{13}\text{ A}$$

**Superposition (with opposite signs):**

$$I_4 = I_{4,1} - I_{4,2} = \frac{18}{13} - \frac{6}{13} = \frac{12}{13}\text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_4 \approx 0.923\text{ A}}$$

**(b)** [7 Marks] **State and prove** the Superposition Theorem for a linear bilateral network containing two independent sources.

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** *(as given in SECTION 1)*

**Proof:**

Let the total response be $Y$. For a linear network, $Y$ is a linear function of the independent sources $S_1$ and $S_2$:

$$Y(S_1, S_2) = a S_1 + b S_2$$

where $a$ and $b$ are network constants.

When $S_2 = 0$ (deactivated): $Y_1 = a S_1$.

When $S_1 = 0$ (deactivated): $Y_2 = b S_2$.

When both sources act:

$$Y_{total} = a S_1 + b S_2 = Y_1 + Y_2$$

Hence, the total response equals the algebraic sum of the individual responses. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice for Internal Option]

**(a)** [7 Marks] In the network shown, find the **voltage across the $5\ \Omega$ resistor** using the **Superposition Theorem**. Given: $I_s = 4\text{ A}$ (current source), $V_s = 10\text{ V}$ (voltage source), $R_1 = 5\ \Omega$ in series with $I_s$, $R_2 = 10\ \Omega$ in series with $V_s$, and the $5\ \Omega$ load resistor is connected across the output terminals.

**Model Solution:**

**Stage 1 — Activate $I_s$ only (Open $V_s$):**

The $V_s$ branch is opened, so the circuit consists of $I_s$ feeding $R_1$ in series with the parallel combination of $R_2$ and the $5\ \Omega$ load.

$$R_p = \frac{10 \times 5}{10 + 5} = \frac{50}{15} = \frac{10}{3}\ \Omega$$

$$R_{total,1} = R_1 + R_p = 5 + \frac{10}{3} = \frac{25}{3}\ \Omega$$

**Voltage across the load (using current divider principle and Ohm's Law):**

The total current from $I_s$ splits between $R_2$ and $R_{load}$:

$$I_{load,1} = I_s \times \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_{load}} = 4 \times \frac{10}{10 + 5} = 4 \times \frac{10}{15} = \frac{8}{3}\text{ A}$$

$$V_{5\Omega,1} = I_{load,1} \times 5 = \frac{8}{3} \times 5 = \frac{40}{3}\text{ V} \approx 13.33\text{ V}$$

**Stage 2 — Activate $V_s$ only (Open $I_s$):**

The current source is opened. The circuit is now $V_s$ in series with $R_2$ and the load (which is in parallel with $R_1$ — but $R_1$ is dangling at one end since $I_s$ is open; so effectively $R_1$ is out of the loop).

$$R_{total,2} = R_2 + R_{load} = 10 + 5 = 15\ \Omega$$

$$I_{load,2} = \frac{V_s}{R_{total,2}} = \frac{10}{15} = \frac{2}{3}\text{ A}$$

$$V_{5\Omega,2} = I_{load,2} \times 5 = \frac{2}{3} \times 5 = \frac{10}{3}\text{ V} \approx 3.33\text{ V}$$

**Superposition:**

Assuming both sources drive current in the **same direction** through the load:

$$V_{5\Omega} = V_{5\Omega,1} + V_{5\Omega,2} = \frac{40}{3} + \frac{10}{3} = \frac{50}{3}\text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{5\Omega} \approx 16.67\text{ V}}$$

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain **why Superposition Theorem cannot be used to find the power dissipated in a resistor** in a circuit with multiple sources.

**Model Solution:**

Power is related to voltage and current by a **quadratic** (non-linear) law:

$$P = V \cdot I = \frac{V^2}{R} = I^2 R$$

Consider a resistor $R$ with current $I = I_1 + I_2$ from two sources. The actual power is:

$$P_{actual} = I^2 R = (I_1 + I_2)^2 R = I_1^2 R + 2 I_1 I_2 R + I_2^2 R$$

$$P_{actual} = P_1 + P_2 + 2 I_1 I_2 R$$

The **cross-term** $2 I_1 I_2 R \neq 0$ in general, meaning:

$$P_{actual} \neq P_1 + P_2$$

Therefore, **superposition is invalid for power**. The correct procedure is to first find the total current or voltage using superposition, **then** compute the power from that total. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Mark Losers!)**
> 1. **Dependent source blunder** (–2 marks): Students mistakenly short/open **dependent sources**. **NEVER** deactivate them. They must remain in the circuit.
> 2. **Sign convention error** (–2 marks): When adding partial responses, students often add magnitudes without checking direction. Always use a **defined reference direction** and assign **+ / –** signs rigorously.
> 3. **Power calculation trap** (–3 marks): Computing $P_1 + P_2$ directly is the most common mistake. Always find $I_{total}$ or $V_{total}$ first, **then** calculate $P$.
> 4. **Forgetting to restore the source** (–2 marks): When deactivating a source for the $k$-th stage, ensure all other sources are properly replaced; the $k$-th source must be in its original form.
> 5. **Applying to non-linear elements** (–4 marks): The theorem fails for diodes, transistors in saturation, iron-core inductors with saturation. Always verify linearity first.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Superposition Theorem**

- **Definition:** Total response = algebraic sum of individual source responses.
- **Voltage source OFF** $\Rightarrow$ Replace with **short circuit** ($V = 0$).
- **Current source OFF** $\Rightarrow$ Replace with **open circuit** ($I = 0$).
- **Dependent sources** $\Rightarrow$ **Never** deactivated; **always retained**.
- **Sign Convention:** Strictly use KVL/KCL-defined reference directions; algebraic addition must respect **+ and –** signs.
- **Number of Stages:** $n$ independent sources $\Rightarrow$ $n$ sub-circuits to solve.
- **Power Exception:** Superposition **fails for power**; compute $I_{total}$ or $V_{total}$ first, then $P = I^2 R$.
- **Linearity is Mandatory:** Non-linear elements $\Rightarrow$ theorem invalid.
- **Bilateral Property:** Theorem requires the network to be **bilateral** (characteristics symmetric in both directions).
- **Verification Tool:** Always cross-check with **Mesh** or **Nodal** analysis for full marks in KTU ESE.
- **Common Use Case:** Multi-source DC circuits, AC circuits with phasors, and small-signal amplifier analysis.
- **Time-Saving Tip:** Combine parallel/series reductions **before** deactivating any source to minimize calculation effort.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocity Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEIT303 - NETWORK THEORY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reciprocity Theorem # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reciprocity Theorem (KTU Canonical Statement)** > In any linear, bilateral, time-invariant, and passive network containing only **independent sources** and **impedances (R, L, C)**, the ratio of excitation (voltage/current source) applied at one port to the response (current/voltage) obtained at another port remains constant when the positions of excitation and response are interchanged. The theorem can be stated in **two dual forms**: | Form | Excitation | Response | Statement | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Voltage Reciprocity | Voltage Source $V$ | Current $I$ | If $V$ in branch A produces $I$ in branch B, then moving $V$ to branch B produces the same $I$ in branch A. | | Current Reciprocity | Current Source $I_s$ | Voltage $V$ | If $I_s$ in branch A produces $V$ in branch B, then moving $I_s$ to branch B produces the same $V$ in branch A. | The mathematical compact form (Voltage Reciprocity) is: $$\frac{V_1}{I_2} = \frac{V_2}{I_1}$$ where $V_1$ and $I_2$ are the source and response in the *original* network, and $V_2$ and $I_1$ are the source and response in the *reciprocal* network. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Two-Way Conversation" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Echo Tube** > Imagine you are speaking into one end of a long hollow tube, and a friend at the other end hears you at a certain **loudness**. The tube acts as a medium. Now swap places — your friend speaks with the same effort, and you hear them at the **exact same loudness**. The medium (the tube) treats forward and backward signals symmetrically. The Reciprocity Theorem is the electrical equivalent of this physical symmetry. A linear passive network is "symmetric" in the sense that if signal A can travel from Port 1 to Port 2 with a certain gain, the *same* signal can travel from Port 2 to Port 1 with the **same gain**. The network has no built-in "preferred direction" of signal flow. **Engineering Intuition Checklist:** - **Linear:** Ohm's law holds (response is proportional to excitation). - **Bilateral:** Components like resistors, inductors, and capacitors conduct equally in both directions. The theorem **fails** for unilateral elements like diodes and transistors (unless used in their linear, small-signal region). - **Passive:** No controlled (dependent) sources — they often *violate* reciprocity unless treated carefully. - **Independent sources only:** The theorem applies when we have a single independent source moving between ports. ## 1.3 Units and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - The ratio $V/I$ carries units of **Ohms ($\Omega$)** — formally called the **Transfer Impedance** $Z_{transfer}$. > - Reciprocity asserts $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, where subscripts denote the transfer between ports. > - In pure resistive networks, this ratio is the **Transfer Resistance** $R_{12} = R_{21}$. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Conditions of Validity The Reciprocity Theorem is valid **only if every one** of the following conditions is satisfied: 1. The network must be **linear** — all parameters ($R$, $L$, $C$) are constant, independent of the applied excitation. 2. The network must be **bilateral** — current flows with equal ease in both directions through every element. 3. The network must be **passive** — energy is not generated internally by dependent/independent sources during the response measurement (only **one independent source** is permitted as the excitation). 4. Initial conditions on energy-storage elements ($L$ and $C$) must be **zero** at $t = 0^-$. 5. The network is **time-invariant** — component values do not drift with time. 6. **Switching operation** must not occur between the original and reciprocal configurations (network topology stays identical). > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Misconception:** Students often believe that dependent sources automatically invalidate reciprocity. This is **not strictly true** — a network with dependent sources *can* be reciprocal, but it requires $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ to hold. Reciprocity is a property of the **two-port network** as a whole, not of individual elements. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Below is the consolidated formula sheet for solving Reciprocity Theorem problems in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). All these formulas appear directly in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCEIT303 Module 1. | Symbol / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Voltage Reciprocity (Core Identity) | $\dfrac{V_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{V_2}{I_1}$ | $\Omega$ | Ratio of source voltage to response current is invariant. | | Current Reciprocity (Dual Identity) | $\dfrac{I_{s1}}{V_2} = \dfrac{I_{s2}}{V_1}$ | $\text{S}$ (Siemens) | Ratio of source current to response voltage is invariant. | | Transfer Impedance (Original) | $Z_{12} = \dfrac{V_1}{I_2}$ | $\Omega$ | Impedance "seen" by moving source and response. | | Transfer Impedance (Reciprocal) | $Z_{21} = \dfrac{V_2}{I_1}$ | $\Omega$ | Same as $Z_{12}$ if the theorem holds. | | Two-Port Reciprocity Condition | $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ | $\Omega$ | Formal two-port network reciprocity test. | | Y-Parameter Reciprocity | $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ | $\text{S}$ | Admittance parameter symmetry. | | Mesh Reciprocity (Bilateral) | $Z_{\text{mesh},12} = Z_{\text{mesh},21}$ | $\Omega$ | Off-diagonal mesh impedance symmetry. | | Power Equivalence | $P_1 = P_2$ | $\text{W}$ | Power delivered to response branch is the same in both cases. | | Admittance Form (Current Reciprocity) | $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ | $\text{S}$ | Equivalent formulation in $Y$-parameters. | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Reciprocity Theorem is **not just an academic curiosity** — it is foundational to several engineering fields: - **Antenna Engineering:** The **Reciprocity Theorem of antennas** states that the receive and transmit patterns of an antenna are identical. This is why a Wi-Fi antenna's radiation pattern (in transmit mode) is the same as its reception sensitivity pattern (in receive mode). - **Network Analyzers:** Two-port VNA (Vector Network Analyzer) measurements rely on the assumption that $S_{12} = S_{21}$ for reciprocal devices under test. - **Power Systems:** In transmission line analysis, reciprocity helps simplify fault analysis and load-flow studies. - **Audio and Acoustics:** The acoustic transfer function between two points in a room is symmetric — the same principle extends to electrical networks. - **Telecommunications:** Used in **filter design** to verify symmetric passband characteristics. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Proof of Reciprocity Theorem (Voltage Form) We prove the theorem rigorously using **mesh analysis** for a generic two-mesh linear bilateral network. This is the standard KTU 2024 Scheme derivation. ### Setup Consider a two-mesh linear bilateral network with self-impedances $Z_{11}$ and $Z_{22}$, and mutual (shared) impedance $Z_m$. Let a voltage source $V_1$ be placed in **Mesh 1** and we measure the current $I_2$ flowing in **Mesh 2**. > [!NOTE] > The mesh impedance matrix for a bilateral network is **symmetric** because the mutual impedance between mesh $i$ and mesh $j$ is the same irrespective of order. Hence, we can denote the off-diagonal term uniformly as $Z_m$. ### Original Network Mesh Equations Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** to the two meshes: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Mesh 1:} \quad & Z_{11} \cdot I_1 + Z_m \cdot I_2 = V_1 \\ \text{Mesh 2:} \quad & Z_m \cdot I_1 + Z_{22} \cdot I_2 = 0 \end{aligned}$$ > *Textual Explanation:* The self-impedance of Mesh 1 multiplied by its mesh current plus the mutual impedance (shared branch) multiplied by the adjacent mesh current equals the source voltage in Mesh 1. Mesh 2 has no source. ### Solving for the Response Current $I_2$ (Original) From the second equation, we express $I_1$ in terms of $I_2$: $$\begin{aligned} Z_m \cdot I_1 + Z_{22} \cdot I_2 & = 0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad I_1 & = -\dfrac{Z_{22}}{Z_m} \cdot I_2 \end{aligned}$$ Substituting this $I_1$ expression into the first mesh equation: $$\begin{aligned} Z_{11} \cdot \left( -\dfrac{Z_{22}}{Z_m} \cdot I_2 \right) + Z_m \cdot I_2 & = V_1 \\ -\dfrac{Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}}{Z_m} \cdot I_2 + Z_m \cdot I_2 & = V_1 \end{aligned}$$ Factor out $I_2$: $$\begin{aligned} I_2 \cdot \left( Z_m - \dfrac{Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}}{Z_m} \right) & = V_1 \\ I_2 \cdot \left( \dfrac{Z_m^2 - Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}}{Z_m} \right) & = V_1 \end{aligned}$$ Solving explicitly for $I_2$: $$I_2 = \dfrac{V_1 \cdot Z_m}{Z_m^2 - Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}}$$ This is the response current in the **original** configuration. ### Reciprocal Network Mesh Equations Now we **interchange the source and the response**: place the source $V_2$ in **Mesh 2** and measure the current $I_1'$ in **Mesh 1** (denoted with a prime to distinguish from the original network currents). The mesh equations become: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Mesh 1:} \quad & Z_{11} \cdot I_1' + Z_m \cdot I_2' = 0 \\ \text{Mesh 2:} \quad & Z_m \cdot I_1' + Z_{22} \cdot I_2' = V_2 \end{aligned}$$ > *Textual Explanation:* The roles of source and response are now swapped. Mesh 1 has no source, and Mesh 2 has the source $V_2$. ### Solving for the Response Current $I_1'$ (Reciprocal) From the first equation of the reciprocal network: $$\begin{aligned} Z_{11} \cdot I_1' + Z_m \cdot I_2' & = 0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad I_2' & = -\dfrac{Z_{11}}{Z_m} \cdot I_1' \end{aligned}$$ Substituting into the second mesh equation: $$\begin{aligned} Z_m \cdot I_1' + Z_{22} \cdot \left( -\dfrac{Z_{11}}{Z_m} \cdot I_1' \right) & = V_2 \\ Z_m \cdot I_1' - \dfrac{Z_{22} \cdot Z_{11}}{Z_m} \cdot I_1' & = V_2 \end{aligned}$$ Factor out $I_1'$: $$I_1' \cdot \left( \dfrac{Z_m^2 - Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}}{Z_m} \right) = V_2$$ Solving explicitly for $I_1'$: $$I_1' = \dfrac{V_2 \cdot Z_m}{Z_m^2 - Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}}$$ ### Final Reciprocity Identity Now we form the two transfer ratios and compare: $$\begin{aligned} \dfrac{V_1}{I_2} & = \dfrac{V_1}{\left( \dfrac{V_1 \cdot Z_m}{Z_m^2 - Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}} \right)} = \dfrac{Z_m^2 - Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}}{Z_m} \\ \dfrac{V_2}{I_1'} & = \dfrac{V_2}{\left( \dfrac{V_2 \cdot Z_m}{Z_m^2 - Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}} \right)} = \dfrac{Z_m^2 - Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}}{Z_m} \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\dfrac{V_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{V_2}{I_1'}}$$ This proves the **Voltage Reciprocity Theorem**. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Two-Port Parameter Form (Analytical Matrix) For a general linear two-port network, the Reciprocity Theorem takes an elegant matrix form. Define the **Z-parameters** (open-circuit impedance parameters): $$\begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} & = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ The reciprocal condition demands: $$Z_{12} = Z_{21}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Result:** For a reciprocal two-port, the **Z-matrix is symmetric**. This is a compact, examinable form of the theorem that KTU examiners love to test in the 14-mark questions. Similarly, in **Y-parameters** (short-circuit admittance parameters): $$Y_{12} = Y_{21}$$ > [!NOTE] > Note the **duality**: $Z$ reciprocity requires symmetric Z-matrix; $Y$ reciprocity requires symmetric Y-matrix. These are mathematically equivalent statements for passive reciprocal networks. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Original vs Reciprocal Network The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the conceptual swap of excitation and response between the **Original Network (N)** and the **Reciprocal Network (N')**.
