RecTest --> RecYes: YES RecTest --> RecNo: NO RecYes: NETWORK IS RECIPROCAL RecNo: NETWORK IS NON RECIPROCAL RecYes --> [*] RecNo --> [*] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 1]** **State the Reciprocity Theorem for linear bilateral networks. Mention any three conditions for its validity.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Statement (1 Mark):** In a linear, bilateral, passive, time-invariant network, the ratio of the excitation (voltage or current source) applied at one port to the response (current or voltage) measured at another port remains the **same** even when the positions of excitation and response are **interchanged**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Validity Conditions (2 Marks):** > 1. The network must be **linear** (parameters independent of excitation magnitude). > 2. The network must be **bilateral** (current flows equally well in both directions). > 3. The network must be **passive** and contain only **one independent source** as excitation. Initial energy in storage elements must be zero. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam, Module 1]** **Define Transfer Impedance. How is it related to the Reciprocity Theorem?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (1.5 Marks):** **Transfer Impedance** $Z_{12}$ between two ports of a network is defined as the ratio of the voltage applied at one port (with the other port open-circuited) to the current flowing into the other port (with the first port open-circuited): > $$Z_{12} = \dfrac{V_1}{I_2} \Big\vert_{I_1 = 0}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Relation to Reciprocity (1.5 Marks):** The Reciprocity Theorem in two-port form states that the transfer impedance is **symmetric**, i.e., $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$. This means the impedance "seen" looking from Port 1 to Port 2 is identical to that from Port 2 to Port 1. Mathematically, the Z-matrix of a reciprocal two-port is **symmetric**: > $$Z = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{12} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, S3 B.Tech ESE]** **(a)** **[7 Marks]** In the network shown in the figure (a $T$-network with $Z_A = 2 \Omega$, $Z_B = 4 \Omega$, $Z_C = 6 \Omega$), a $12\,\text{V}$ source is connected across the left branch (Port 1) and the short-circuit current at the right branch (Port 2) is measured. Using the Reciprocity Theorem, find the current that would flow if the $12\,\text{V}$ source is moved to the right branch (Port 2) and the short-circuit is placed at the left branch (Port 1). Verify your answer using mesh analysis. **(b)** **[7 Marks]** For the same $T$-network, compute the Z-parameters and verify the symmetry condition $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$. --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Applying the Reciprocity Theorem [7 Marks] **Step 1: Setup the T-Network Equations (Original Configuration)** Let the T-network have series arm $Z_A = 2\,\Omega$ (left), shunt arm $Z_B = 4\,\Omega$ (middle), and series arm $Z_C = 6\,\Omega$ (right). Let Port 1 be on the left and Port 2 be on the right. Original network: Source $V_1 = 12\,\text{V}$ at Port 1, short-circuit at Port 2. > [!NOTE] > When Port 2 is short-circuited, the shunt arm $Z_B$ is in **parallel** with $Z_C$ (since the right end of $Z_C$ is now connected to the short, effectively in parallel with $Z_B$ through the source return path... actually, more carefully: $Z_B$ and $Z_C$ are both connected between the central node and ground/return, so they are in **parallel**). The equivalent impedance seen by the source $V_1$ is: $$Z_{\text{eq}} = Z_A + (Z_B \parallel Z_C)$$ Compute $Z_B \parallel Z_C$: $$Z_B \parallel Z_C = \dfrac{Z_B \cdot Z_C}{Z_B + Z_C} = \dfrac{4 \times 6}{4 + 6} = \dfrac{24}{10} = 2.4\,\Omega$$ Total impedance: $$Z_{\text{eq}} = 2 + 2.4 = 4.4\,\Omega$$ Current from the source (entering Port 1): $$I_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{V_1}{Z_{\text{eq}}} = \dfrac{12}{4.4} = \dfrac{30}{11} \approx 2.727\,\text{A}$$ This current splits between $Z_B$ and $Z_C$ at the central node. Using the **current divider rule**, the current through $Z_C$ (which is the current at Port 2) is: $$I_2 = I_{\text{total}} \cdot \dfrac{Z_B}{Z_B + Z_C} = \dfrac{30}{11} \cdot \dfrac{4}{4 + 6} = \dfrac{30}{11} \cdot \dfrac{4}{10} = \dfrac{120}{110} = \dfrac{12}{11}\,\text{A}$$ > *Textual Explanation:* Since $Z_B$ and $Z_C$ are in parallel, the current divides inversely proportional to their resistances. The current through $Z_C$ is the short-circuit current at Port 2. **Step 2: Apply Reciprocity Theorem (Reciprocal Configuration)** In the reciprocal configuration, the source $V_2 = 12\,\text{V}$ is moved to Port 2, and Port 1 is short-circuited. By the Reciprocity Theorem: $$\dfrac{V_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{V_2}{I_1'}$$ Since $V_1 = V_2 = 12\,\text{V}$, we conclude: $$I_1' = I_2 = \dfrac{12}{11}\,\text{A} \approx 1.0909\,\text{A}$$ > [!NOTE] > **The Reciprocity Theorem guarantees the current at Port 1 in the reciprocal network equals $I_2 = 12/11\,\text{A}$.** **Step 3: Verify via Mesh Analysis (Original Network) [2 Marks]** Mesh equations for the original network. Define mesh 1 (left loop with source) and mesh 2 (right loop, short-circuited). The current $I_2$ in mesh 2 equals the short-circuit current at Port 2. Self-impedances and mutual impedances: - $Z_{11} = Z_A + Z_B = 2 + 4 = 6\,\Omega$ - $Z_{22} = Z_B + Z_C = 4 + 6 = 10\,\Omega$ - $Z_m = Z_B = 4\,\Omega$ (shared branch) Mesh equations: $$\begin{aligned} 6 \cdot I_1 + 4 \cdot I_2 & = 12 \\ 4 \cdot I_1 + 10 \cdot I_2 & = 0 \end{aligned}$$ From the second equation: $$I_1 = -\dfrac{10}{4} \cdot I_2 = -2.5 \cdot I_2$$ Substituting into the first: $$\begin{aligned} 6 \cdot (-2.5 \cdot I_2) + 4 \cdot I_2 & = 12 \\ -15 \cdot I_2 + 4 \cdot I_2 & = 12 \\ -11 \cdot I_2 & = 12 \\ I_2 & = -\dfrac{12}{11}\,\text{A} \end{aligned}$$ The magnitude matches: $\vert I_2 \vert = 12/11\,\text{A}$, and the negative sign simply indicates direction. ✓ #### Part (b) — Z-Parameter Computation and Symmetry Verification [7 Marks] **Step 1: Compute $Z_{11}$ (Port 2 Open, Excite Port 1)** Apply $I_1$ at Port 1 with Port 2 open. The impedance seen is: $$Z_{11} = Z_A + Z_B = 2 + 4 = 6\,\Omega$$ **Step 2: Compute $Z_{21}$ (Port 2 Open, Excite Port 1, Measure Port 2 Voltage)** The voltage at Port 2 is the voltage across $Z_C$. Since no current flows through $Z_C$ (Port 2 is open), the central node voltage is $V_C = I_1 \cdot Z_B$ and the Port 2 voltage is $V_2 = V_C = I_1 \cdot Z_B$ (since no drop across $Z_C$). $$Z_{21} = \dfrac{V_2}{I_1} = Z_B = 4\,\Omega$$ **Step 3: Compute $Z_{22}$ (Port 1 Open, Excite Port 2)** Apply $I_2$ at Port 2 with Port 1 open. The impedance seen is: $$Z_{22} = Z_C + Z_B = 6 + 4 = 10\,\Omega$$ **Step 4: Compute $Z_{12}$ (Port 1 Open, Excite Port 2, Measure Port 1 Voltage)** The voltage at Port 1 is the voltage across $Z_A$. Since no current flows through $Z_A$ (Port 1 is open), the Port 1 voltage equals the central node voltage: $V_1 = I_2 \cdot Z_B$. $$Z_{12} = \dfrac{V_1}{I_2} = Z_B = 4\,\Omega$$ **Step 5: Verify Symmetry** $$\boxed{Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 4\,\Omega \quad \text{(Reciprocity Confirmed ✓)}}$$ The Z-matrix is: $$Z = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 4 \\ 4 & 10 \end{bmatrix}\,\Omega$$ This is symmetric, confirming the network is reciprocal. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, S3 B.Tech ESE, Alternative Choice]** **(a)** **[7 Marks]** State and prove the Reciprocity Theorem for a linear bilateral two-mesh network using mesh analysis. Clearly write down the assumptions and the final identity obtained. **(b)** **[7 Marks]** For a $\pi$-network with $Y_A = 0.1\,\text{S}$ (left shunt), $Y_B = 0.2\,\text{S}$ (right shunt), and series arm impedance $Z_s = 5\,\Omega$, calculate the Y-parameters and show that $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$. --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Statement and Proof [7 Marks] **Statement (2 Marks):** As given in Section 1.1 of these notes — see the formal KTU definition. **Proof (5 Marks):** The detailed proof is given in Section 3.1 of these notes. The student must reproduce: $$\begin{aligned} & \text{Original:} \quad I_2 = \dfrac{V_1 \cdot Z_m}{Z_m^2 - Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}} \\ & \text{Reciprocal:} \quad I_1' = \dfrac{V_2 \cdot Z_m}{Z_m^2 - Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}} \\ & \therefore \quad \dfrac{V_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{V_2}{I_1'} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ The student should explicitly state that the **symmetry of the off-diagonal mesh impedance** ($Z_m$ being the same regardless of mesh order) is what makes the two expressions equal. #### Part (b) — $\pi$-Network Y-Parameter Computation [7 Marks] **Step 1: Identify the $\pi$-Network Topology** A $\pi$-network has two shunt admittances and one series impedance: - $Y_A = 0.1\,\text{S}$ at Port 1 (left) - $Y_B = 0.2\,\text{S}$ at Port 2 (right) - $Z_s = 5\,\Omega$ (series middle), so $Y_s = 1/Z_s = 0.2\,\text{S}$ **Step 2: Compute $Y_{11}$ (Port 2 Short-Circuited, Excite Port 1)** When Port 2 is shorted, the series admittance $Y_s$ is in parallel with $Y_B$: $$Y_s + Y_B = 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.4\,\text{S}$$ The total admittance seen at Port 1 is: $$Y_{11} = Y_A + (Y_s + Y_B) = 0.1 + 0.4 = 0.5\,\text{S}$$ **Step 3: Compute $Y_{21}$ (Port 2 Short-Circuited, Measure Port 2 Current)** With Port 2 shorted, applying $V_1$ at Port 1, the current entering the shorted Port 2 is the current through $Y_s$: $$I_2 = -V_1 \cdot Y_s = -V_1 \cdot 0.2$$ (The negative sign arises from the standard Y-parameter sign convention where $I_2$ flows **out** of Port 2.) $$Y_{21} = \dfrac{I_2}{V_1} = -Y_s = -0.2\,\text{S}$$ **Step 4: Compute $Y_{22}$ (Port 1 Short-Circuited, Excite Port 2)** By symmetry of the computation, when Port 1 is shorted: $$Y_{22} = Y_B + (Y_s + Y_A) = 0.2 + (0.2 + 0.1) = 0.5\,\text{S}$$ **Step 5: Compute $Y_{12}$ (Port 1 Short-Circuited, Measure Port 1 Current)** Applying $V_2$ at Port 2 with Port 1 shorted, the current entering the shorted Port 1 is: $$I_1 = -V_2 \cdot Y_s = -V_2 \cdot 0.2$$ $$Y_{12} = \dfrac{I_1}{V_2} = -Y_s = -0.2\,\text{S}$$ **Step 6: Verify Symmetry** $$\boxed{Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -0.2\,\text{S} \quad \text{(Reciprocity Confirmed ✓)}}$$ The Y-matrix is: $$Y = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.2 \\ -0.2 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{S}$$ This matrix is **symmetric**, confirming the $\pi$-network is reciprocal. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Reciprocity Theorem Problems (KTU Examiner Insights):** > > 1. **Forgetting the Initial Conditions Check (2-3 marks lost):** Students jump directly to the theorem without stating that initial energy in $L$ and $C$ is zero. KTU examiners specifically check this line. > > 2. **Misidentifying the Response Branch (3-4 marks lost):** When the theorem says "current in branch CD," students often measure voltage or confuse the response with the source. Always re-read the problem statement to identify what is the *excitation* and what is the *response*. > > 3. **Violating the Single-Source Rule (4-5 marks lost):** If the network has more than one independent source, the Reciprocity Theorem **cannot be applied directly**. Students must either use superposition first or fall back to mesh/nodal analysis. > > 4. **Sign Convention Errors in Mesh Analysis Verification (2 marks lost):** The negative sign in $I_2 = -12/11\,\text{A}$ indicates direction. Students who write $\vert I_2 \vert = 12/11$ without commenting on direction lose 1 mark for the sign convention. > > 5. **Using the Theorem on Networks with Dependent Sources Without Verification (5+ marks lost):** Dependent sources can produce non-reciprocal networks. The student must either explicitly verify $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ or use a generic two-port test, not blindly invoke the theorem. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reciprocity Theorem (Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme)** - **Core Identity:** $\dfrac{V_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{V_2}{I_1'}$ (Voltage Form) and $\dfrac{I_{s1}}{V_2} = \dfrac{I_{s2}}{V_1'}$ (Current Form). - **Validity Requirements:** Linear + Bilateral + Passive + Single Independent Source + Zero Initial Conditions + Time-Invariant. - **Two-Port Formulation:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ (Z-matrix symmetric) and $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ (Y-matrix symmetric). - **Proof Tool:** Mesh analysis (or nodal analysis) with off-diagonal symmetry $Z_m$ (or $Y_m$) being the same in both directions. - **Network Class:** Reciprocity holds for **passive RLC networks**, transmission lines, antennas, and most linear filters. It **fails** for circuits with diodes, transistors (in saturation/cutoff), gyrators, and circulators. - **Antenna Reciprocity:** The transmit and receive radiation patterns of any linear antenna are identical — a direct application of the Reciprocity Theorem in electromagnetics. - **KTU Frequently Tested Concepts:** (i) State the theorem with conditions. (ii) Solve a 2-mesh network numerically. (iii) Verify reciprocity via Z or Y parameter symmetry. (iv) Identify whether a given network is reciprocal. - **Power Equivalence:** Power delivered to the response branch is the **same** in both original and reciprocal configurations (when source magnitudes are equal). - **Dual Theorem:** The **Thevenin-Norton duality** mirrors the dual structure of voltage and current reciprocity forms. - **Examiner's Golden Rule:** Always state the conditions of validity *before* applying the theorem — this single line is worth 1-2 marks in every KTU 14-mark question.

topics.title: Maximum Power Transfer Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIT303 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maximum Power Transfer Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (MPTT)** is a fundamental result in electrical network analysis that dictates the condition under which a load, connected to a two-terminal linear bilateral network (modeled as a Thevenin or Norton equivalent), draws the **maximum possible real (average) power** from the source.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the theorem is stated as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (DC / Resistive Case):** *In a linear bilateral network, the maximum power is delivered to the load resistance $R_L$ when the load resistance is equal to the Thevenin equivalent resistance $R_{Th}$ of the source network as seen from the load terminals.*
>
> **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (AC / Impedance Case):** *In a sinusoidal steady-state linear network, maximum average power is transferred to a load impedance $Z_L$ when $Z_L$ is the complex conjugate of the Thevenin impedance $Z_{Th}$ of the source network, i.e., $Z_L = Z_{Th}^*$.*

Where the **complex conjugate** operation flips the sign of the imaginary part: if $Z_{Th} = R_{Th} + jX_{Th}$, then $Z_L = R_{Th} - jX_{Th}$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are an electrical engineer designing a **water pump system** that pushes water through a pipeline to drive a turbine (the load).

* The **source (pump + pipes + valves)** has a fixed internal friction (analogous to Thevenin resistance $R_{Th}$).
* The **turbine (load)** has a controllable resistance to water flow (analogous to $R_L$).
* If the turbine offers **too little resistance**, the water rushes through too fast, but the turbine blades cannot catch enough force — most of the pump's energy is wasted as turbulence (heat dissipated in $R_{Th}$).
* If the turbine offers **too much resistance**, the water barely moves, so very little energy is delivered.
* There exists **one perfect "sweet spot"** where the turbine's resistance exactly matches the pipe's internal friction, and the turbine extracts the **maximum hydraulic power** from the system.

This "sweet spot" in electrical networks is the **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem**. The source network is reduced to its Thevenin equivalent $(V_{Th}, R_{Th})$, and we tune $R_L$ until it equals $R_{Th}$.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Important Quantities

> [!NOTE]
> **Key quantities to remember (always carry correct units):**
> * **Load Power $P_L$** — measured in **Watts (W)**.
> * **Thevenin Voltage $V_{Th}$** — measured in **Volts (V)**.
> * **Thevenin Resistance $R_{Th}$ / Impedance $Z_{Th}$** — measured in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**.
> * **Load Resistance $R_L$ / Impedance $Z_L$** — measured in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**.
> * **Source Efficiency $\eta$ at MPTT** — exactly **50%** for the DC resistive case and **at most 50%** for the AC case (equals 50% only when $X_{Th} = 0$).

### 1.4 Conditions of Validity

The theorem is universally applied in KTU board problems under these implicit conditions:

* The source network is **linear and bilateral** (contains only linear elements: $R$, $L$, $C$, transformers, dependent sources, but **no non-linear** diodes or transistors in saturation).
* The load is **purely passive** for the DC case ($R_L > 0$).
* For AC analysis, the network is in **sinusoidal steady state** (phasor domain applies).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Case Framework

KTU examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between two scenarios, and marks are routinely lost when these are mixed up. The framework is:

#### Case A — Purely Resistive Load (DC or Resistive AC network)

The source is reduced to a Thevenin equivalent: an ideal voltage source $V_{Th}$ in series with $R_{Th}$. The load $R_L$ is a simple resistor.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Reduce** the source network (with $R_L$ removed, terminals open) to find $V_{Th}$ and $R_{Th}$.
2. **Reconnect** $R_L$ across the Thevenin terminals.
3. **Compute** the load current using Ohm's law:
$$I_L = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$$
4. **Compute** the power delivered to the load:
$$P_L = I_L^2 \, R_L = \frac{V_{Th}^{\,2} \, R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2}$$
5. **Optimize** by taking the derivative $\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0$ to find the optimum $R_L$.

#### Case B — Complex Impedance Load (Sinusoidal AC)

The source is reduced to a phasor Thevenin equivalent $\mathbf{V}_{Th}$ with impedance $Z_{Th} = R_{Th} + jX_{Th}$. The load $Z_L = R_L + jX_L$ has independently controllable real and imaginary parts.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. Reduce the network to phasor form.
2. Write the load current phasor:
$$\mathbf{I}_L = \frac{\mathbf{V}_{Th}}{Z_{Th} + Z_L}$$
3. Compute the **average (real) power** delivered to the load:
$$P_L = \frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{I}_L \vert^2 \, R_L = \frac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2 \, R_L}{2 \vert (R_{Th} + R_L) + j(X_{Th} + X_L) \vert^2}$$
4. **Optimize** the imaginary part first (set $X_L = -X_{Th}$ to minimize the denominator's magnitude).
5. **Optimize** the real part second (set $R_L = R_{Th}$).

### 2.2 Why the Theorem Matters in Real Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical applications where MPTT is the design goal:**
> * **Communication electronics** — radio, TV, and cellular receivers are designed so antenna/signal-source impedances match the input impedance of the front-end amplifier (typically $50\ \Omega$) for maximum signal-power transfer.
> * **Audio systems** — loudspeaker impedance matching networks (transformers, autotransformers, crossover networks).
> * **Instrumentation** — signal-conditioning front ends and transducer readouts (e.g., maximizing power from a thermocouple or strain gauge).
> * **Antenna tuning units** — to ensure maximum radiated signal power from a transmitter to its antenna.
>
> **Critical caveat:** MPTT is *not* used in high-power applications (power transmission lines, electric vehicle drives, power grids) because the **50% efficiency** would waste enormous energy. There, **impedance matching for minimum loss** is the design objective (impedances kept as small as practical).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Condition | Formula | Valid For | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load current (resistive case) | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$ | DC / Resistive | A |
| 2 | Power delivered to load (resistive) | $P_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}^{\,2} \, R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2}$ | DC / Resistive | W |
| 3 | **MPTT condition (resistive)** | $\boxed{R_L = R_{Th}}$ | DC / Resistive | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | **Maximum power (resistive)** | $\boxed{P_{L,\max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^{\,2}}{4 R_{Th}}}$ | DC / Resistive | W |
| 5 | **Efficiency at MPTT (resistive)** | $\eta_{MPTT} = 50\%$ | DC / Resistive | % |
| 6 | Load current phasor (AC) | $\mathbf{I}_L = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}_{Th}}{Z_{Th} + Z_L}$ | AC | A |
| 7 | Average power to load (AC) | $P_L = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert^2 \, R_L}{2 \left[(R_{Th}+R_L)^2 + (X_{Th}+X_L)^2\right]}$ | AC | W |
| 8 | **MPTT condition (AC, conjugate match)** | $\boxed{Z_L = Z_{Th}^\*}$ | AC | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | **Maximum average power (AC)** | $\boxed{P_{L,\max} = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert^2}{4 R_{Th}}}$ | AC | W |
| 10 | **Maximum efficiency (AC, general)** | $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{R_L}{R_{Th} + R_L} \times 100\% = 50\%$ at full MPTT | AC | % |
| 11 | Half-power ($-3$ dB) condition | $R_L = R_{Th}\left(1 \pm \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | DC | $\Omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vertical bars $\vert \cdot \vert$ above denote the **magnitude** of a phasor (i.e., $\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert$ is the RMS or peak amplitude as appropriate). KTU's valuation expects students to **explicitly state the magnitude**, especially for sinusoidal sources where the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor (peak-based) vs no factor (RMS-based) routinely causes 1–2 mark deductions.

### 2.4 Pitfall — RMS vs Peak Convention

KTU problems use **two conventions** for AC phasor amplitude:

* **Peak convention:** $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$, average power = $\frac{1}{2} I_m^2 R_L$.
* **RMS convention:** $v(t) = \sqrt{2}\, V_{rms} \cos(\omega t + \phi)$, average power = $I_{rms}^2 R_L$.

Both yield **identical** $P_{L,\max} = V_{Th,\text{rms}}^{\,2} / (4 R_{Th})$ or $V_{Th,\text{peak}}^{\,2} / (8 R_{Th})$. Always confirm the convention from the problem statement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (Resistive Case)

**Setup:** The Thevenin equivalent of the source network provides $V_{Th}$ in series with $R_{Th}$. The load $R_L$ is connected across the open terminals.

**Step 1 — Express the load current using Ohm's law:**

The current flowing through the series loop of $V_{Th}$, $R_{Th}$, and $R_L$ is:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$$

**Step 2 — Express the power dissipated in the load:**

Using the relation $P_L = I_L^2 \, R_L$:

$$P_L = \left(\frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}\right)^2 \cdot R_L = \frac{V_{Th}^{\,2} \, R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Optimize by differentiation:**

For a maximum, set the first derivative $\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0$. Apply the quotient rule. Let $N = V_{Th}^{\,2} R_L$ and $D = (R_{Th} + R_L)^2$, so $P_L = N/D$.

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = \frac{N' \cdot D - N \cdot D'}{D^2} = \frac{V_{Th}^{\,2}(R_{Th} + R_L)^2 - V_{Th}^{\,2} R_L \cdot 2(R_{Th} + R_L)}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^4}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

Factor out $V_{Th}^{\,2}(R_{Th} + R_L)$ from the numerator:

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = \frac{V_{Th}^{\,2} (R_{Th} + R_L)\left[(R_{Th} + R_L) - 2 R_L\right]}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^4} = \frac{V_{Th}^{\,2} \left[R_{Th} - R_L\right]}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^3}$$

**Step 5 — Set the derivative to zero:**

For the optimum:

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_{Th} - R_L = 0$$

$$\boxed{R_L = R_{Th}}$$

This is the **Maximum Power Transfer condition** for the resistive case.

**Step 6 — Confirm it's a maximum (second derivative test):**

For $R_L < R_{Th}$, the numerator $(R_{Th} - R_L) > 0$, so $\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} > 0$ (function increasing). For $R_L > R_{Th}$, $\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} < 0$ (function decreasing). Hence the critical point is indeed a **maximum**.

**Step 7 — Compute the maximum power:**

Substitute $R_L = R_{Th}$ into the power expression:

$$P_{L,\max} = \frac{V_{Th}^{\,2} \cdot R_{Th}}{(R_{Th} + R_{Th})^2} = \frac{V_{Th}^{\,2} \cdot R_{Th}}{4 R_{Th}^{\,2}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{L,\max} = \frac{V_{Th}^{\,2}}{4 R_{Th}}}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the efficiency at MPTT:**

Total power supplied by source = $V_{Th} \cdot I_L = V_{Th} \cdot \dfrac{V_{Th}}{2R_{Th}} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^{\,2}}{2 R_{Th}}$.

$$\eta = \frac{P_L}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{V_{Th}^{\,2} / (4 R_{Th})}{V_{Th}^{\,2} / (2 R_{Th})} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

So at MPTT, **half the source power is wasted** in $R_{Th}$, confirming the 50% efficiency result.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (AC, Complex Impedance Case)

**Setup:** Phasor source $\mathbf{V}_{Th}$ in series with $Z_{Th} = R_{Th} + jX_{Th}$. Load $Z_L = R_L + jX_L$ with $R_L \geq 0$.

**Step 1 — Load current phasor:**

$$\mathbf{I}_L = \frac{\mathbf{V}_{Th}}{(R_{Th} + R_L) + j(X_{Th} + X_L)}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude squared of the current:**

$$\vert \mathbf{I}_L \vert^2 = \frac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert^2}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2 + (X_{Th} + X_L)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Average real power delivered to load (only the resistive part of $Z_L$ dissipates):**

$$P_L = \frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{I}_L \vert^2 \, R_L = \frac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert^2 \, R_L}{2 \left[(R_{Th} + R_L)^2 + (X_{Th} + X_L)^2\right]}$$

**Step 4 — Optimize with respect to $X_L$ (imaginary part first):**

Notice that $X_L$ appears only in the denominator and only as a squared term. To **minimize** the denominator (and hence **maximize** $P_L$):

$$X_{Th} + X_L = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{X_L = -X_{Th}}$$

This zeroes out the reactive part of the denominator, which is a necessary condition for MPTT in AC.

**Step 5 — Substitute back and optimize with respect to $R_L$:**

With $X_L = -X_{Th}$, the power expression reduces to:

$$P_L = \frac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert^2 \, R_L}{2 (R_{Th} + R_L)^2}$$

This is mathematically identical to the DC case (up to the factor $\frac{1}{2}$). Applying the same derivative procedure as in Derivation I:

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{R_L = R_{Th}}$$

**Step 6 — Combine the two conditions:**

$$Z_L = R_L + jX_L = R_{Th} - jX_{Th} = (R_{Th} + jX_{Th})^* = Z_{Th}^*$$

$$\boxed{Z_L = Z_{Th}^*}$$

This is the **conjugate matching condition** for MPTT in AC circuits.

**Step 7 — Compute the maximum average power:**

Substitute $R_L = R_{Th}$ and $X_L = -X_{Th}$ into the power expression:

$$P_{L,\max} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert^2 \cdot R_{Th}}{2 (R_{Th} + R_{Th})^2} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert^2 \cdot R_{Th}}{2 \cdot 4 R_{Th}^{\,2}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{L,\max} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert^2}{4 R_{Th}}}$$

**Note:** The maximum power formula in AC has the **same algebraic form** as the DC case, but it represents **average** power, and the denominator $R_{Th}$ is the **resistive part** of $Z_{Th}$ (not $\vert Z_{Th} \vert$).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Board Style Problem (Resistive)

**Problem:** A source network has a Thevenin equivalent of $V_{Th} = 24$ V and $R_{Th} = 6\ \Omega$. Find (a) the value of load resistance $R_L$ for maximum power transfer, and (b) the maximum power delivered to the load.

**Solution:**

**(a)** By MPTT for the resistive case:
$$R_L = R_{Th} = 6\ \Omega$$

**(b)** Maximum power:
$$P_{L,\max} = \frac{V_{Th}^{\,2}}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{(24)^2}{4 \times 6} = \frac{576}{24} = 24\ \text{W}$$

The total power supplied by the source = $V_{Th}^2 / (2 R_{Th}) = 576 / 12 = 48$ W, confirming the 50% efficiency.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Board Style Problem (AC, Phasor)

**Problem:** The Thevenin equivalent at the load terminals of an amplifier is $\mathbf{V}_{Th} = 10 \angle 0^\circ$ V (RMS) and $Z_{Th} = (4 + j3)\ \Omega$. Find the load impedance $Z_L$ for maximum power transfer and compute $P_{L,\max}$.

**Solution:**

The conjugate of $Z_{Th}$ is:
$$Z_{Th}^* = (4 - j3)\ \Omega$$

By MPTT for AC:
$$Z_L = Z_{Th}^* = (4 - j3)\ \Omega$$

The magnitude of the source voltage (RMS): $\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert = 10$ V.

Maximum average power:
$$P_{L,\max} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert^2}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{(10)^2}{4 \times 4} = \frac{100}{16} = 6.25\ \text{W}$$

The current at MPTT is $\mathbf{I}_L = \mathbf{V}_{Th} / (Z_{Th} + Z_L) = 10 / (8 + j0) = 1.25$ A, which gives $P_L = (1.25)^2 \times 4 = 6.25$ W. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thevenin Equivalent with Load (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram shows the reduced Thevenin source driving the load, which is the canonical model used in every MPTT problem.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[VTh Source] -->|Series RTh| B(Thevenin Terminals)
    B --> C[Load ZL]
    B --> D[Open Circuit Voltage VTh]
    B --> E[Short Circuit Current ISC]
    F[Compute RL = RTh] --> C
    G[Compute ZL = ZTh star] --> C
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — MPTT Decision Procedure for KTU Problems

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start Given Network] --> B{Is the load ZL a single resistor or a complex impedance?}
    B -->|Resistor RL| C[Find Thevenin VTh and RTh at load terminals]
    B -->|Complex ZL| D[Find Thevenin VTh phasor and ZTh phasor]
    C --> E[Set RL equal to RTh]
    E --> F[Compute PL max as VTh squared over 4 RTh]
    D --> G[Set ZL equal to ZTh complex conjugate]
    G --> H[Compute PL max as VTh mag squared over 4 RTh real]
    F --> I[Report answer with units]
    H --> I[Report answer with units]
```

### 4.3 Power vs Load Resistance Curve (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Load Resistance RL] --> Y[Power PL]
    Y --> P0[PL at RL 0 equals 0 W]
    Y --> P1[PL at RL equal to RTh equals VTh squared over 4 RTh peak]
    Y --> P2[PL at RL infinity equals 0 W]
```

This curve illustrates the bell-shaped behaviour of load power. The peak occurs at the unique point $R_L = R_{Th}$.

### 4.4 Source vs Load Power Distribution at MPTT

```mermaid
graph TD
    SP[Source Power Delivered by VTh] --> SP1[Power Dissipated in RTh equals 50 percent]
    SP --> SP2[Power Delivered to Load RL equals 50 percent]
    SP1 --> W1[Waste Heat in Source Resistance]
    SP2 --> W2[Useful Power to Load]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above intentionally avoid embedding markdown formatting inside the mermaid labels. Mermaid's parser can fail to render diagrams if bold, italics, or special characters appear inside double-quoted node labels. The labels are kept in plain uppercase text for maximum compatibility.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem for a DC network. Mention the efficiency at maximum power transfer.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Maximum Power Transfer Theorem states that in a linear bilateral network, maximum power is delivered to a variable load resistance $R_L$ when the load resistance is equal to the Thevenin equivalent resistance $R_{Th}$ of the source network as seen from the load terminals, i.e., $R_L = R_{Th}$.
>
> The maximum power delivered is $P_{L,\max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^{\,2}}{4 R_{Th}}$.
>
> The efficiency of power transfer at this condition is exactly **50%**, meaning half the source power is dissipated as heat in the source's internal resistance $R_{Th}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State the condition for maximum power transfer in an AC circuit and write the expression for maximum power.**

**Model Answer:**

> For sinusoidal AC steady-state networks, maximum average power is transferred to a complex load impedance $Z_L$ when it is the **complex conjugate** of the Thevenin impedance $Z_{Th}$ of the source network:
> $$\boxed{Z_L = Z_{Th}^*}$$
> If $Z_{Th} = R_{Th} + jX_{Th}$, then $Z_L = R_{Th} - jX_{Th}$.
>
> The maximum average power delivered is:
> $$P_{L,\max} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert^2}{4 R_{Th}}$$
> where $\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert$ is the magnitude of the Thevenin voltage phasor (in peak or RMS form as consistent with the source specification) and $R_{Th}$ is the **real part** of $Z_{Th}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Resistive Network Problem**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

For the network shown in the figure, determine:
**(a)** The value of load resistance $R_L$ for which maximum power is transferred to it. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The corresponding maximum power delivered. **(7 marks)**

*Network description:* A 30 V ideal source in series with a $10\ \Omega$ resistor, this combination is in parallel with a $20\ \Omega$ resistor, and the parallel combination drives the load $R_L$ through a series $5\ \Omega$ resistor.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** Find the Thevenin equivalent across the $R_L$ terminals (with $R_L$ removed).

Step 1 — **Thevenin voltage $V_{Th}$ (open-circuit voltage at the $R_L$ terminals):**
With $R_L$ removed, no current flows through the $5\ \Omega$ series resistor, so the voltage at the $R_L$ terminals is the voltage across the $20\ \Omega$ resistor. Using the voltage divider:

$$V_{Th} = 30 \times \frac{20}{10 + 20} = 30 \times \frac{20}{30} = 20\ \text{V}$$

Step 2 — **Thevenin resistance $R_{Th}$:**
Deactivate the 30 V source (replace with a short circuit). The $10\ \Omega$ and $20\ \Omega$ resistors are now in parallel, and this combination is in series with the $5\ \Omega$ resistor:

$$R_{Th} = (10 \parallel 20) + 5 = \frac{10 \times 20}{10 + 20} + 5 = \frac{200}{30} + 5 = 6.667 + 5 = 11.667\ \Omega$$

Step 3 — **MPTT condition:**
$$\boxed{R_L = R_{Th} = 11.667\ \Omega = \frac{35}{3}\ \Omega}$$

**Part (b):** Maximum power:

$$P_{L,\max} = \frac{V_{Th}^{\,2}}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{(20)^2}{4 \times 11.667} = \frac{400}{46.667} = 8.571\ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{L,\max} \approx 8.57\ \text{W}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is to forget to **deactivate the source** (short-circuit voltage sources, open-circuit current sources) when computing $R_{Th}$. Marks are deducted when the source is left active in the resistance computation. A second common error is to compute the parallel combination incorrectly when dependent sources are present; in such cases, the **test source method** must be used.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — AC Phasor Network Problem**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

The Thevenin equivalent at the load terminals of a single-phase AC source is $\mathbf{V}_{Th} = 50 \angle 30^\circ$ V (RMS) and $Z_{Th} = (30 + j40)\ \Omega$. For maximum power transfer to a variable complex load $Z_L$:

**(a)** Determine the value of $Z_L$ for maximum power transfer. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compute the maximum average power delivered to the load. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** The conjugate of $Z_{Th}$ is:
$$Z_{Th}^* = (30 - j40)\ \Omega$$

By the AC MPTT (conjugate matching):
$$\boxed{Z_L = Z_{Th}^* = (30 - j40)\ \Omega}$$

The magnitude of $Z_L$ is $\vert Z_L \vert = \sqrt{30^2 + 40^2} = 50\ \Omega$.

**Part (b):** The Thevenin voltage magnitude is $\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert = 50$ V (RMS), and the real part of $Z_{Th}$ is $R_{Th} = 30\ \Omega$.

Maximum average power:
$$P_{L,\max} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert^2}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{(50)^2}{4 \times 30} = \frac{2500}{120} = 20.833\ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{L,\max} \approx 20.83\ \text{W}}$$

**Verification:** At MPTT, the total series impedance is $Z_{Th} + Z_L = 30 + j40 + 30 - j40 = 60\ \Omega$ (purely resistive). The RMS load current is $\vert \mathbf{I}_L\vert = 50/60 = 0.833$ A. Power delivered to load = $(0.833)^2 \times 30 = 20.83$ W. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Two errors are commonly penalized in this question type. (1) Students write the load impedance as $Z_L = Z_{Th}$ (no conjugate) — this gives the **minimum power transfer** condition, not the maximum, and is worth zero. (2) Students use $\vert Z_{Th}\vert$ (i.e., $50\ \Omega$) in the denominator of $P_{L,\max}$ instead of the **real part** $R_{Th} = 30\ \Omega$. The correct denominator is always $4 R_{Th}$, **never** $4 \vert Z_{Th}\vert$. Marks for the final numerical answer are lost immediately if this is misused.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Maximum Power Transfer Theorem**
>
> - **Resistive MPTT condition:** $R_L = R_{Th}$, where $R_{Th}$ is the Thevenin resistance seen from the load terminals with the source deactivated.
> - **Resistive maximum power:** $P_{L,\max} = V_{Th}^{\,2} / (4 R_{Th})$.
> - **Resistive efficiency at MPTT:** exactly **50%** — half the source power is lost in $R_{Th}$.
> - **AC MPTT condition (conjugate match):** $Z_L = Z_{Th}^*$. The conjugate flips the sign of the imaginary part: $R_L = R_{Th}$ and $X_L = -X_{Th}$.
> - **AC maximum average power:** $P_{L,\max} = \vert \mathbf{V}_{Th}\vert^2 / (4 R_{Th})$ — uses the **real part** of $Z_{Th}$, not $\vert Z_{Th}\vert$.
> - **Two-step optimization in AC:** first set $X_L = -X_{Th}$ (to zero the reactive part of the denominator), then set $R_L = R_{Th}$.
> - **Units:** $V$ in Volts, $R$ in Ohms, $P$ in Watts, $\vert \mathbf{V}\vert$ in V (RMS or peak consistent with the problem).
> - **Independent sources only?** For dependent sources, the **test source method** is required to compute $R_{Th}$ — short-circuiting dependent sources is not permitted.
> - **Power transmission does NOT use MPTT** — efficiency is too low (50%); such systems design for **minimum loss** with $R_L \gg R_{Th}$.
> - **Communication electronics DOES use MPTT** — maximum signal power transfer from antenna to receiver front-end is the primary design goal.
> - **Common KTU trap:** the maximum power formula $V^2 / (4R)$ looks similar to $V^2 / R$ (total source power). Remember the **factor of 4**, not 1.
> - **Quick sanity check:** if you accidentally swap the roles of $R_{Th}$ and $R_L$ in the formula, your $P_{L,\max}$ value will be wrong by a factor of $(R_{Th}/R_L)$ — always substitute $R_L = R_{Th}$ *before* computing power.
> - **MPTT vs minimum-loss matching:** MPTT gives $R_L = R_{Th}$; minimum-loss (high-power) matching requires $R_L \gg R_{Th}$. Never confuse these two.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steady State AC Analysis: Application of Network Theorems to Solve AC Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIT303 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steady State AC Analysis: Application of Network Theorems to Solve AC Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Steady State AC Analysis** is the study of linear electrical networks operating with sinusoidal time-varying sources after all transient components have decayed to zero. In this regime, every voltage and current in the network oscillates sinusoidally at the **source frequency** ($\omega$ rad/s), and the network's response can be completely described by **complex phasors** rather than time-domain functions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Steady-state AC analysis deals with the behaviour of linear networks excited by sinusoidal sources in the *sinusoidal steady state*, where voltages and currents are represented as phasors (complex numbers), and the role of resistance is replaced by **impedance** $Z = R + jX$."

**Application of Network Theorems to AC Circuits** means extending the classical theorems — **Superposition, Thevenin, Norton, Maximum Power Transfer, Reciprocity, Millman, Tellegen, and Substitution** — from the DC domain (real numbers) to the sinusoidal steady-state domain (complex phasors). The mathematical structure is identical; only the scalar resistances $R$ are promoted to complex **impedances** $Z(\omega)$, and DC sources are replaced by **phasor sources** $\mathbf{V}$ and $\mathbf{I}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phasor Transform Notation (Euler's Identity):**
> A sinusoid $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi_v)$ is mapped to the phasor $\mathbf{V} = V_m \angle \phi_v = V_m e^{j\phi_v}$. Time derivatives become multiplication by $j\omega$, and time integration becomes division by $j\omega$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **lighthouse beam** rotating at a constant angular speed. The beam represents a sinusoidal signal. Now imagine the *brightness* of the beam being captured by a still camera with a long exposure. The camera does not see the rotation — it sees a **frozen snapshot** showing the beam's position (angle) and intensity (magnitude). That frozen snapshot is the **phasor**.

When you apply a network theorem in DC, you are essentially asking: *"If I short this, what voltage appears here?"* The same question in AC asks: *"If I short this, what **phasor voltage** appears here?"* — the algebra is the same, but each quantity now carries a **magnitude AND an angle**, like a vector arrow on the complex plane.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** AC network theorems are the backbone of:
> - **Power systems** — fault analysis, load flow, transformer equivalent circuits
> - **RF/Microwave engineering** — impedance matching, filter design
> - **Audio electronics** — crossover networks, equalizers
> - **Communication systems** — analysing modulated signals in linear front-ends

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Source frequency:** $f$ in **Hz**, with $\omega = 2\pi f$ in **rad/s**
- **Inductor impedance:** $Z_L = j\omega L$  (units: **Ω**)
- **Capacitor impedance:** $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega C}$  (units: **Ω**)
- **Resistor impedance:** $Z_R = R$  (real, frequency-independent)
- **Admittance:** $Y = \dfrac{1}{Z} = G + jB$ in **siemens (S)**
- **Phasor reference:** Pure cosine, $v(t) = \text{Re}\{\mathbf{V} e^{j\omega t}\}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal AC Network Theorem Algorithm

For **every** classical network theorem, the path from the theorem statement to a solution follows this exact 5-stage procedure in the AC domain:

1. **Phasor Conversion Stage** — Convert every sinusoidal source $V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ to a phasor $\mathbf{V} = V_m \angle \phi$. Convert $L \to j\omega L$, $C \to 1/(j\omega C)$, leave $R$ as $R$.
2. **Impedance Construction Stage** — Build the network on the *complex plane*. Resistors remain on the **real axis**; inductors sit on the **+j axis**; capacitors sit on the **−j axis**.
3. **Theorem Application Stage** — Apply the chosen theorem using complex arithmetic (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division of phasors).
4. **Inverse Phasor Conversion Stage** — Convert the resulting phasor answer back to the time domain: $\mathbf{V} \to V_m \cos(\omega t + \angle \mathbf{V})$.
5. **Validation Stage** — Verify KVL, KCL, power balance, and frequency-domain consistency.

## 2.2 Theorem-by-Theorem Operational Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Superposition Theorem (AC Form)

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** In any linear bilateral network containing $n$ independent sinusoidal sources, the steady-state response (voltage or current) at any element equals the **phasor sum** of the responses produced by each source acting *alone* with all other sources deactivated (voltage sources → short, current sources → open).

**Why it works:** Because sinusoidal steady-state analysis is governed by a **system of linear complex equations** (KVL/KCL in phasor form), the principle of linear superposition applies directly in the frequency domain.

**Procedure:**
- Deactivate all sources except one. Compute the partial response $\mathbf{X}_k$.
- Repeat for all $n$ sources.
- Total response: $\mathbf{X}_{total} = \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} \mathbf{X}_k$

### 2.2.2 Thevenin's Theorem (AC Form)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** Any two-terminal linear AC network containing sources and impedances can be replaced by an equivalent circuit consisting of a single **Thevenin phasor voltage** $\mathbf{V}_{Th}$ in series with a single **Thevenin impedance** $Z_{Th}$, where $Z_{Th}$ is computed at the **source frequency** $\omega$.

**Determination of $\mathbf{V}_{Th}$:**
$$\mathbf{V}_{Th} = \mathbf{V}_{OC}$$
where $\mathbf{V}_{OC}$ is the open-circuit phasor voltage measured at the terminals.

**Determination of $Z_{Th}$ — Three equivalent methods:**

| # | Method | Procedure |
|---|--------|-----------|
| 1 | Deactivation | Zero all independent sources; compute the equivalent impedance looking into the terminals. |
| 2 | Short-circuit | Compute short-circuit current $\mathbf{I}_{SC}$ at terminals; then $Z_{Th} = \mathbf{V}_{OC} / \mathbf{I}_{SC}$. |
| 3 | Test source | Apply a fictitious test source $\mathbf{V}_t$ (or $\mathbf{I}_t$) at the terminals; $Z_{Th} = \mathbf{V}_t / \mathbf{I}_t$. |

### 2.2.3 Norton's Theorem (AC Form)

Norton equivalent is the **current-source dual** of the Thevenin equivalent.

$$\mathbf{I}_N = \mathbf{I}_{SC} \quad ; \quad Z_N = Z_{Th}$$

**Source transformation** between the two:
$$\mathbf{V}_{Th} = \mathbf{I}_N \cdot Z_{Th} \quad \text{or} \quad \mathbf{I}_N = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}_{Th}}{Z_{Th}}$$

### 2.2.4 Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (AC Form)

This is the **most subtle** of the AC theorems because the optimum load is generally **complex**.

| Load Type | Optimum Load | Maximum Power Delivered |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------------|
| Purely resistive load | $R_L = \vert Z_{Th} \vert$ (when $Z_{Th}$ is purely real) | $P_{max} = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2}{4 R_{Th}}$ |
| Variable complex load $Z_L = R_L + jX_L$ | $Z_L = Z_{Th}^{\,*} = R_{Th} - jX_{Th}$ (complex conjugate match) | $P_{max} = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2}{4 R_{Th}}$ |
| Load magnitude $\vert Z_L \vert$ variable, angle fixed | $\vert Z_L \vert = \vert Z_{Th} \vert$ | $P = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2 \cdot \vert Z_L \vert \cos\theta_L}{(\vert Z_{Th} \vert^2 + \vert Z_L \vert^2 + 2\vert Z_{Th} \vert \vert Z_L \vert \cos(\theta_{Th} - \theta_L))}$ |

### 2.2.5 Reciprocity Theorem (AC Form)

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** In any linear, bilateral, single-source AC network, the ratio of excitation (source) to response is unchanged when the positions of the source and the measuring element are interchanged.

Mathematically: $\dfrac{\mathbf{V}_s}{\mathbf{I}_m} = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}_s'}{\mathbf{I}_m'}$ where primed quantities are after source/responder interchange.

### 2.2.6 Millman's Theorem (AC Form)

For multiple parallel branches between two nodes:
$$\mathbf{V}_{AB} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} \mathbf{V}_k \, Y_k}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} Y_k}$$
where $\mathbf{V}_k$ is the phasor source in branch $k$ (treated as a Thevenin source with impedance $Z_k$, hence $Y_k = 1/Z_k$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Theorem / Quantity | Phasor Formula | KTU Use |
|---|--------------------|----------------|---------|
| 1 | Phasor transform | $\mathbf{V} = V_m \angle \phi_v$ | Convert sinusoids to phasors |
| 2 | Inductor impedance | $Z_L = j\omega L$ | $X_L = \omega L$ Ω |
| 3 | Capacitor impedance | $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega C}$ | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ Ω |
| 4 | Series impedance | $Z_{eq} = \displaystyle\sum_{k} Z_k$ | Reduces to DC form |
| 5 | Parallel impedance | $Z_{eq} = \left(\displaystyle\sum_{k} \dfrac{1}{Z_k}\right)^{-1}$ | Reduces to DC form |
| 6 | Superposition | $\mathbf{X}_{total} = \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} \mathbf{X}_k$ | Phasor sum |
| 7 | Thevenin voltage | $\mathbf{V}_{Th} = \mathbf{V}_{OC}$ | Open-circuit phasor voltage |
| 8 | Thevenin impedance | $Z_{Th} = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}_{OC}}{\mathbf{I}_{SC}}$ | Complex ratio |
| 9 | Norton current | $\mathbf{I}_N = \mathbf{I}_{SC}$ | Short-circuit phasor current |
| 10 | Source transformation | $\mathbf{V}_{Th} = \mathbf{I}_N \cdot Z_{Th}$ | Bidirectional |
| 11 | Max power (complex load) | $Z_L = Z_{Th}^{\,*}$ | $P_{max} = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2}{4 R_{Th}}$ |
| 12 | Max power (resistive load) | $R_L = R_{Th}$ (when $X_{Th}=0$) | $P_{max} = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}}$ |
| 13 | Millman voltage | $\mathbf{V}_{AB} = \dfrac{\sum \mathbf{V}_k Y_k}{\sum Y_k}$ | Multi-branch parallel |
| 14 | Reciprocity ratio | $\dfrac{\mathbf{V}_1}{\mathbf{I}_1} = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}_2}{\mathbf{I}_2}$ | Single-source networks |
| 15 | Power in AC load | $P = \vert \mathbf{I} \vert^2 R_L$ (W) | Real power delivered |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where AC Network Theorems Power Modern Engineering:**

- **Power Grid Protection Engineers** use Thevenin equivalents to compute fault currents and design relay settings — the entire ANSI/IEEE device numbering scheme (51, 50, 27, etc.) depends on it.
- **RF Antenna Engineers** use Maximum Power Transfer with complex conjugate matching to ensure maximum signal transfer between transmitter and antenna (e.g., a 50 Ω transmitter matched to a 73 Ω antenna through an L-section).
- **Audio Amplifier Designers** use Thevenin/Norton equivalents to model op-amp output stages and pre-amplifier loading.
- **Power Electronics Engineers** use superposition on switching waveforms (Fourier series) to predict harmonic distortion in converters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Thevenin Equivalent of an AC Bridge Network

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Find the Thevenin equivalent circuit (magnitude and phase) looking into terminals **a-b** of the network shown below, then compute the load current and load power for $Z_L = 5 - j3 \, \Omega$. Source: $\mathbf{V}_s = 100 \angle 0^\circ$ V at $\omega = 10^3$ rad/s.

**Network description:**
- Voltage source $\mathbf{V}_s = 100\angle 0^\circ$ V in series with $Z_1 = 5 \, \Omega$ on the left branch.
- Branch 1 (top): $Z_1 = 5 \, \Omega$ (between source and node **a**).
- Branch 2 (top right): $Z_2 = 3 + j4 \, \Omega$ (between node **a** and node **b**).
- Branch 3 (bottom): $Z_3 = 4 - j3 \, \Omega$ (between source return and node **b**).
- Terminals **a** and **b** are the output where the load $Z_L$ is connected.

### Step 1: Construct the phasor impedance network

Convert every element to its phasor-domain equivalent:
- $Z_1 = 5 \angle 0^\circ \, \Omega = 5 + j0 \, \Omega$
- $Z_2 = 3 + j4 \, \Omega = 5 \angle 53.13^\circ \, \Omega$
- $Z_3 = 4 - j3 \, \Omega = 5 \angle -36.87^\circ \, \Omega$
- $\mathbf{V}_s = 100 \angle 0^\circ$ V

### Step 2: Compute Thevenin voltage $\mathbf{V}_{Th} = \mathbf{V}_{OC}$

With terminals a-b **open**, no current flows out. Therefore the same current $\mathbf{I}$ circulates through $Z_1$, $Z_2$, and $Z_3$ (they form a single series loop).

Compute total loop impedance:
$$Z_{loop} = Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_3$$

$$Z_{loop} = 5 + (3 + j4) + (4 - j3) = (5+3+4) + j(4-3) = 12 + j1 \, \Omega$$

Convert to polar:
$$\vert Z_{loop} \vert = \sqrt{12^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{145} = 12.0416 \, \Omega$$

$$\angle Z_{loop} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{12}\right) = 4.7636^\circ$$

So $Z_{loop} = 12.0416 \angle 4.7636^\circ \, \Omega$.

Loop current:
$$\mathbf{I}_{loop} = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}_s}{Z_{loop}} = \dfrac{100 \angle 0^\circ}{12.0416 \angle 4.7636^\circ} = 8.3046 \angle -4.7636^\circ \text{ A}$$

The voltage across $Z_3$ (which is $\mathbf{V}_{OC}$ between a-b, since a-b is in parallel with $Z_3$ when open):

$$\mathbf{V}_{Th} = \mathbf{I}_{loop} \cdot Z_3$$

$$\mathbf{V}_{Th} = (8.3046 \angle -4.7636^\circ) \cdot (5 \angle -36.87^\circ)$$

$$\mathbf{V}_{Th} = 41.523 \angle (-4.7636 - 36.87)^\circ = 41.523 \angle -41.6336^\circ \text{ V}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Inverse phasor conversion (time domain):**
> $v_{Th}(t) = 41.523 \cos(10^3 t - 41.63^\circ)$ V

### Step 3: Compute Thevenin impedance $Z_{Th}$

Deactivate the source: replace $\mathbf{V}_s$ with a **short circuit**. The impedance seen from a-b is $Z_2$ in **parallel** with $(Z_1 + Z_3)$ (since the shorted source puts $Z_1$ and $Z_3$ in series, and that series combination is in parallel with $Z_2$).

**Branch 1: $Z_1 + Z_3$**
$$Z_1 + Z_3 = 5 + (4 - j3) = 9 - j3 \, \Omega$$

**Convert to polar for division:**
$$\vert Z_1 + Z_3 \vert = \sqrt{9^2 + (-3)^2} = \sqrt{90} = 9.4868 \, \Omega$$

$$\angle(Z_1 + Z_3) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{-3}{9}\right) = -18.4349^\circ$$

**Parallel combination:**
$$Z_{Th} = \dfrac{Z_2 \cdot (Z_1 + Z_3)}{Z_2 + (Z_1 + Z_3)}$$

Compute numerator (in polar):
$$Z_2 \cdot (Z_1 + Z_3) = (5 \angle 53.13^\circ)(9.4868 \angle -18.4349^\circ) = 47.434 \angle 34.6951^\circ$$

Compute denominator (in rectangular first):
$$Z_2 + (Z_1 + Z_3) = (3 + j4) + (9 - j3) = 12 + j1 \, \Omega$$

In polar:
$$\vert 12 + j1 \vert = 12.0416 \quad ; \quad \angle = \tan^{-1}(1/12) = 4.7636^\circ$$

Now divide:
$$Z_{Th} = \dfrac{47.434 \angle 34.6951^\circ}{12.0416 \angle 4.7636^\circ} = 3.9395 \angle 29.9315^\circ \, \Omega$$

**Back to rectangular for clarity:**
$$Z_{Th} = 3.9395 \cos(29.9315^\circ) + j\, 3.9395 \sin(29.9315^\circ)$$
$$Z_{Th} \approx 3.4129 + j\, 1.9658 \, \Omega$$

### Step 4: Compute load current with $Z_L = 5 - j3 \, \Omega$

The Thevenin equivalent circuit is $\mathbf{V}_{Th}$ in series with $Z_{Th}$ feeding the load $Z_L$.

Total series impedance:
$$Z_{total} = Z_{Th} + Z_L = (3.4129 + j1.9658) + (5 - j3)$$
$$Z_{total} = 8.4129 - j1.0342 \, \Omega$$

In polar:
$$\vert Z_{total} \vert = \sqrt{8.4129^2 + 1.0342^2} = \sqrt{71.8181} = 8.4763 \, \Omega$$

$$\angle Z_{total} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{-1.0342}{8.4129}\right) = -7.0096^\circ$$

Load current:
$$\mathbf{I}_L = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}_{Th}}{Z_{total}} = \dfrac{41.523 \angle -41.6336^\circ}{8.4763 \angle -7.0096^\circ} = 4.8983 \angle -34.624^\circ \text{ A}$$

### Step 5: Compute power delivered to load

Real power in load:
$$P_L = \vert \mathbf{I}_L \vert^2 \cdot R_L = (4.8983)^2 \cdot 5 = 24.0 \cdot 5 = 120.0 \text{ W}$$

(approximately — exact value to be reported based on calculator precision)

## 3.2 Maximum Power Transfer — Derivation of the Conjugate Match Condition

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show mathematically that for a fixed Thevenin source $\mathbf{V}_{Th}$ and $Z_{Th} = R_{Th} + jX_{Th}$, the load $Z_L = R_L + jX_L$ that absorbs maximum average real power satisfies $Z_L = Z_{Th}^{\,*}$.

**Setup:** The load current phasor:
$$\mathbf{I}_L = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}_{Th}}{(R_{Th} + R_L) + j(X_{Th} + X_L)}$$

Magnitude squared:
$$\vert \mathbf{I}_L \vert^2 = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2 + (X_{Th} + X_L)^2}$$

Real power absorbed by $R_L$:
$$P_L = \vert \mathbf{I}_L \vert^2 \cdot R_L = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2 \cdot R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2 + (X_{Th} + X_L)^2}$$

**Optimisation — two partial derivatives set to zero:**

**Step A: Optimise $X_L$ (denominator has $X_L^2$ term).** For any fixed $R_L$, the denominator is minimised (and hence $P_L$ maximised) when:
$$X_L = -X_{Th} \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_L \text{ cancels } X_{Th} \text{ reactance}$$

**Step B: Optimise $R_L$ with $X_L = -X_{Th}$.** Substitute:
$$P_L = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2 \cdot R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2}$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $R_L$:
$$\dfrac{dP_L}{dR_L} = \vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2 \cdot \dfrac{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2 - R_L \cdot 2(R_{Th} + R_L)}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^4} = 0$$

Numerator equals zero when:
$$(R_{Th} + R_L)^2 = 2 R_L (R_{Th} + R_L)$$
$$(R_{Th} + R_L) = 2 R_L$$
$$R_{Th} = R_L$$

**Combined optimal condition:**
$$R_L = R_{Th} \quad \text{and} \quad X_L = -X_{Th} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_L = R_{Th} - jX_{Th} = Z_{Th}^{\,*}$$

**Maximum power at this optimum:**
$$P_{max} = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2 \cdot R_{Th}}{(2 R_{Th})^2} = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2}{4 R_{Th}}$$

This is the famous **quarter-power formula** for the conjugate-matched AC load.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Theorem

| Network Signature | Recommended Theorem | Why |
|-------------------|---------------------|-----|
| Multiple independent sources of *different frequencies* | **Superposition** (frequency by frequency) | Phasor superposition only works at a single $\omega$ |
| Load is variable; we want to find $I_L$ as a function of $Z_L$ | **Thevenin** | One-shot equivalent circuit |
| We want to find $R_L$ for $P_{max}$ | **Thevenin + Maximum Power Transfer** | Compute $Z_{Th}$, then $Z_L = Z_{Th}^{\,*}$ |
| Two sources, simple network | **Superposition** | Avoids solving 2 simultaneous KVLs |
| Multiple parallel branches feeding a common node | **Millman** | Single-formula voltage computation |
| Single-source network, exchange source and meter | **Reciprocity** | Avoids re-solving the network |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: AC Network Theorem Solution Flow

The diagram below maps the complete end-to-end **solution architecture** for applying any network theorem to an AC circuit. It serves as a top-down visual master-template that a student can follow for every exam problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START: AC Network Problem]) --> B{Time Domain or Phasor Domain?}
    B -->|Time domain input| C[Convert sources to phasors: Vms angle phi, Is angle theta]
    B -->|Phasor domain input| D[Skip conversion step]
    C --> E[Replace L with j omega L, C with 1 over j omega C, R stays R]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Draw frequency-domain impedance network on complex plane]
    F --> G{Which theorem fits?}
    G -->|Multiple sources| H[Apply SUPERPOSITION: sum partial responses]
    G -->|Single variable load| I[Apply THEVENIN: find Vth and Zth]
    G -->|Need current source form| J[Apply NORTON: In equals Vth over Zth]
    G -->|Need optimal load| K[Apply MAX POWER TRANSFER: ZL equals Zth conjugate]
    G -->|Parallel branches| L[Apply MILLMAN: Vab equals sum VkYk over sum Yk]
    G -->|Single source, swap source and meter| M[Apply RECIPROCITY: ratio is invariant]
    H --> N[Combine partial phasor responses]
    I --> O[Build Thevenin equivalent: Vth in series with Zth]
    J --> O
    K --> P[Solve for IL and PL using final equivalent]
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Convert result phasor back to time domain: Vm cos omega t plus phi]
    Q --> R[Validate: KVL, KCL, power balance, frequency consistency]
    R --> S([END: Final answer with units and phase])
```

## 4.2 Phasor-Plane Geometry: Impedance Mapping

This diagram shows how each AC circuit element maps to a point/region on the **complex impedance plane**. It is essential for visualising how series/parallel combinations behave in the phasor domain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ImZ["COMPLEX IMPEDANCE PLANE Z = R + jX"]
        direction TB
        AX([+jX: Inductive Region]):::ind
        RX([Origin: 0,0]):::orig
        CAP([-jX: Capacitive Region]):::cap
    end
    R1[Resistor: R on real axis] --> RX
    L1[Inductor: j omega L on +j axis] --> AX
    C1[Capacitor: 1 over j omega C on -j axis] --> CAP
    R1 -.->|Real part| RX
    L1 -.->|Imaginary positive| AX
    C1 -.->|Imaginary negative| CAP
    classDef ind fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,color:#000
    classDef orig fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#add8e6,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Thevenin Equivalent Visualisation (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TH["THEVENIN EQUIVALENT (Phasor Domain)"]
        VTH([Phasor Source Vth angle phi_th]):::src
        ZTH[Impedance Zth = Rth + jXth]:::imp
    end
    subgraph LOAD["LOAD TERMINALS"]
        ZL[Load ZL = RL + jXL]:::ld
    end
    VTH -->|series| ZTH
    ZTH -->|terminals a, b| ZL
    classDef src fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef imp fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef ld fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Maximum Power Transfer Condition Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Given: Source Vth and Zth]) --> B[Compute Zth in rectangular: Rth + jXth]
    B --> C[Form complex conjugate: Zth star equals Rth - jXth]
    C --> D{Type of load allowed?}
    D -->|Complex ZL allowed| E[Set ZL equals Zth star for absolute maximum power]
    D -->|Only R L variable| F[Set RL equals Rth to cancel reactive part of Zth]
    E --> G[Pmax equals Vth squared over 4 Rth]
    F --> H[P equals Vth squared RL over Rth plus RL squared]
    G --> I([End: Optimal load identified])
    H --> I
```
