# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`

**Q:** State Thevenin's theorem as applied to sinusoidal steady-state AC circuits. How does it differ from its DC counterpart?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Thevenin's theorem for AC circuits states that any two-terminal linear network containing sinusoidal sources and impedances, operating in sinusoidal steady state, can be replaced by an equivalent circuit consisting of a single phasor voltage source $\mathbf{V}_{Th}$ in series with a single equivalent impedance $Z_{Th}$, where $\mathbf{V}_{Th}$ is the open-circuit phasor voltage at the terminals and $Z_{Th}$ is the impedance looking into the terminals with all independent sources deactivated.
>
> **Difference from DC form (1 mark):** In DC analysis, the theorem uses real-valued voltage $V_{Th}$ and resistance $R_{Th}$. In AC analysis, both are **complex phasors** $\mathbf{V}_{Th}$ and **complex impedances** $Z_{Th}$, carrying both magnitude and phase. The DC form is a special case of the AC form at $\omega = 0$.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`

**Q:** For an AC source with Thevenin equivalent $\mathbf{V}_{Th} = 50\angle 30^\circ$ V and $Z_{Th} = 10 + j5 \, \Omega$, determine the load impedance for maximum power transfer and the corresponding maximum power.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> For maximum power transfer, the load impedance must be the **complex conjugate** of the Thevenin impedance:
> $$Z_L = Z_{Th}^{\,*} = 10 - j5 \, \Omega$$
>
> The maximum real power delivered to the load:
> $$P_{max} = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2}{4 R_{Th}} = \dfrac{50^2}{4 \times 10} = \dfrac{2500}{40} = 62.5 \text{ W}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Format

### Question Choice A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** For the AC network shown in Figure Q-A, find the Thevenin equivalent circuit across terminals **a-b**. Source: $\mathbf{V}_s = 50\angle 0^\circ$ V at $\omega = 10^4$ rad/s. Network: $Z_1 = 4 + j3 \, \Omega$ (left branch), $Z_2 = 6 - j8 \, \Omega$ (right branch top), $Z_3 = 5 \, \Omega$ (right branch bottom), with $Z_1$ in series with the parallel combination of $Z_2$ and $Z_3$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Thevenin equivalent from part (a), determine the current through and power delivered to a load $Z_L = 2 + j1 \, \Omega$ connected across a-b. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) Solution — Thevenin Equivalent

**Step 1: Phasor-domain network**

Given data:
- $\mathbf{V}_s = 50\angle 0^\circ$ V
- $Z_1 = 4 + j3 \, \Omega = 5\angle 36.87^\circ \, \Omega$
- $Z_2 = 6 - j8 \, \Omega = 10\angle -53.13^\circ \, \Omega$
- $Z_3 = 5 \, \Omega = 5\angle 0^\circ \, \Omega$

**Step 2: Find $\mathbf{V}_{Th} = \mathbf{V}_{OC}$**

With terminals a-b open, the circuit forms a single series loop $\mathbf{V}_s \to Z_1 \to Z_2 \to$ back to source. No current flows through $Z_3$ (open terminals). Thus:

$$\mathbf{I}_{loop} = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}_s}{Z_1 + Z_2} = \dfrac{50\angle 0^\circ}{(4 + j3) + (6 - j8)} = \dfrac{50\angle 0^\circ}{10 - j5}$$

Denominator in polar:
$$\vert 10 - j5 \vert = \sqrt{100 + 25} = \sqrt{125} = 11.1803$$
$$\angle(10 - j5) = \tan^{-1}(-5/10) = -26.565^\circ$$

$$\mathbf{I}_{loop} = \dfrac{50\angle 0^\circ}{11.1803 \angle -26.565^\circ} = 4.4721 \angle 26.565^\circ \text{ A}$$

$\mathbf{V}_{Th}$ is the voltage across $Z_2$ (which equals $\mathbf{V}_{ab}$ when a-b is open and $Z_3$ is in parallel with $Z_2$ but no current through it):

$$\mathbf{V}_{Th} = \mathbf{I}_{loop} \cdot Z_2 = (4.4721 \angle 26.565^\circ)(10 \angle -53.13^\circ)$$
$$\mathbf{V}_{Th} = 44.721 \angle -26.565^\circ \text{ V}$$

In rectangular form:
$$\mathbf{V}_{Th} = 44.721\cos(-26.565^\circ) + j\, 44.721\sin(-26.565^\circ) = 40 - j20 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Find $Z_{Th}$**

Deactivate the voltage source (short it). Looking into a-b: $Z_2$ is in parallel with $(Z_1 + Z_3)$ (since the shorted source puts $Z_1$ in series with $Z_3$ from a-b's perspective).

**Series branch:**
$$Z_1 + Z_3 = (4 + j3) + 5 = 9 + j3 \, \Omega$$

In polar: $\vert 9 + j3 \vert = \sqrt{81 + 9} = \sqrt{90} = 9.4868$, $\angle = \tan^{-1}(3/9) = 18.435^\circ$.

**Parallel with $Z_2$:**
$$Z_{Th} = \dfrac{Z_2 (Z_1 + Z_3)}{Z_2 + (Z_1 + Z_3)} = \dfrac{(6 - j8)(9 + j3)}{(6 - j8) + (9 + j3)}$$

Numerator:
$$(6 - j8)(9 + j3) = 54 + j18 - j72 - j^2 24 = 54 + j18 - j72 + 24 = 78 - j54$$

Denominator:
$$(6 - j8) + (9 + j3) = 15 - j5$$

Division in rectangular:
$$Z_{Th} = \dfrac{78 - j54}{15 - j5}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by conjugate of denominator $(15 + j5)$:
$$Z_{Th} = \dfrac{(78 - j54)(15 + j5)}{(15 - j5)(15 + j5)} = \dfrac{78 \cdot 15 + 78 \cdot j5 - j54 \cdot 15 - j^2 54 \cdot 5}{225 + 25}$$
$$= \dfrac{1170 + j390 - j810 + 270}{250} = \dfrac{1440 - j420}{250}$$
$$= 5.76 - j1.68 \, \Omega$$

**Final Thevenin equivalent:**
$$\boxed{\mathbf{V}_{Th} = 40 - j20 \text{ V} \quad ; \quad Z_{Th} = 5.76 - j1.68 \, \Omega}$$

#### Part (b) Solution — Load Current and Power

**Step 1: Total series impedance**
$$Z_{total} = Z_{Th} + Z_L = (5.76 - j1.68) + (2 + j1) = 7.76 - j0.68 \, \Omega$$

In polar:
$$\vert Z_{total} \vert = \sqrt{7.76^2 + 0.68^2} = \sqrt{60.2176 + 0.4624} = \sqrt{60.68} = 7.7897$$
$$\angle Z_{total} = \tan^{-1}(-0.68/7.76) = -5.011^\circ$$

**Step 2: Load current**
$$\mathbf{I}_L = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}_{Th}}{Z_{total}} = \dfrac{40 - j20}{7.76 - j0.68}$$

Convert $\mathbf{V}_{Th}$ to polar: $\vert 40 - j20 \vert = \sqrt{1600 + 400} = \sqrt{2000} = 44.721$, $\angle = \tan^{-1}(-20/40) = -26.565^\circ$.

$$\mathbf{I}_L = \dfrac{44.721 \angle -26.565^\circ}{7.7897 \angle -5.011^\circ} = 5.7411 \angle -21.554^\circ \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Real power in load**
$$P_L = \vert \mathbf{I}_L \vert^2 \cdot R_L = (5.7411)^2 \cdot 2 = 32.96 \cdot 2 = 65.92 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{I}_L = 5.7411 \angle -21.554^\circ \text{ A} \quad ; \quad P_L \approx 65.92 \text{ W}}$$

---

### Question Choice B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Using **Superposition theorem**, find the current through the $4 \, \Omega$ resistor in the AC circuit where two sources act simultaneously: $\mathbf{V}_1 = 20\angle 0^\circ$ V (in series with $Z_{a} = 2 + j3 \, \Omega$ feeding node X), and $\mathbf{V}_2 = 10\angle 90^\circ$ V (in series with $Z_{b} = 3 - j1 \, \Omega$ feeding the same node X), and the $4 \, \Omega$ resistor is connected from node X to ground. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Verify your answer to part (a) using **Norton's theorem** applied to the same network, and compute the Norton current and Norton impedance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) Solution — Superposition

**Source 1 alone (deactivate Source 2 → short-circuit $\mathbf{V}_2$):**

With $\mathbf{V}_2$ shorted, $Z_b = 3 - j1 \, \Omega$ is in parallel with the $4 \, \Omega$ resistor, both fed by $\mathbf{V}_1$ in series with $Z_a = 2 + j3 \, \Omega$.

Equivalent impedance seen by $\mathbf{V}_1$:
$$Z_{eq,1} = Z_a + \left[4 \parallel (3 - j1)\right]$$
$$4 \parallel (3 - j1) = \dfrac{4(3 - j1)}{4 + (3 - j1)} = \dfrac{12 - j4}{7 - j1}$$
$$= \dfrac{(12 - j4)(7 + j1)}{(7 - j1)(7 + j1)} = \dfrac{84 + j12 - j28 + 4}{49 + 1} = \dfrac{88 - j16}{50} = 1.76 - j0.32 \, \Omega$$

So $Z_{eq,1} = (2 + j3) + (1.76 - j0.32) = 3.76 + j2.68 \, \Omega$.

Total current from $\mathbf{V}_1$:
$$\mathbf{I}_{tot,1} = \dfrac{20\angle 0^\circ}{3.76 + j2.68}$$

In polar: $\vert 3.76 + j2.68 \vert = \sqrt{14.1376 + 7.1824} = \sqrt{21.32} = 4.6174$, $\angle = \tan^{-1}(2.68/3.76) = 35.47^\circ$.

$$\mathbf{I}_{tot,1} = \dfrac{20\angle 0^\circ}{4.6174 \angle 35.47^\circ} = 4.3313 \angle -35.47^\circ \text{ A}$$

Current divider to find current through the $4 \, \Omega$ resistor:
$$\mathbf{I}_{4,1} = \mathbf{I}_{tot,1} \cdot \dfrac{Z_b}{Z_b + 4} = (4.3313\angle -35.47^\circ) \cdot \dfrac{3 - j1}{7 - j1}$$

Numerator: $3 - j1 = \sqrt{10}\angle -18.435^\circ = 3.1623\angle -18.435^\circ$.
Denominator: $7 - j1 = \sqrt{50}\angle -8.130^\circ = 7.0711\angle -8.130^\circ$.

$$\dfrac{3 - j1}{7 - j1} = \dfrac{3.1623}{7.0711}\angle (-18.435 + 8.130)^\circ = 0.4472\angle -10.305^\circ$$

$$\mathbf{I}_{4,1} = 4.3313 \cdot 0.4472 \angle (-35.47 - 10.305)^\circ = 1.9370 \angle -45.775^\circ \text{ A}$$

**Source 2 alone (deactivate Source 1 → short-circuit $\mathbf{V}_1$):**

By symmetry of the topology, $Z_a = 2 + j3$ is in parallel with the $4 \, \Omega$ resistor, both fed by $\mathbf{V}_2$ in series with $Z_b = 3 - j1 \, \Omega$.

$$Z_a \parallel 4 = \dfrac{(2 + j3) \cdot 4}{(2 + j3) + 4} = \dfrac{8 + j12}{6 + j3} = \dfrac{(8 + j12)(6 - j3)}{36 + 9} = \dfrac{48 - j24 + j72 + 36}{45} = \dfrac{84 + j48}{45} = 1.8667 + j1.0667 \, \Omega$$

So $Z_{eq,2} = Z_b + (Z_a \parallel 4) = (3 - j1) + (1.8667 + j1.0667) = 4.8667 + j0.0667 \, \Omega$.

In polar: $\vert 4.8667 + j0.0667 \vert \approx 4.8672$, $\angle \approx 0.785^\circ$.

$$\mathbf{I}_{tot,2} = \dfrac{10\angle 90^\circ}{4.8672 \angle 0.785^\circ} = 2.0545 \angle 89.215^\circ \text{ A}$$

Current through the $4 \, \Omega$ resistor (by current divider):
$$\mathbf{I}_{4,2} = \mathbf{I}_{tot,2} \cdot \dfrac{Z_a}{Z_a + 4} = (2.0545\angle 89.215^\circ) \cdot \dfrac{2 + j3}{6 + j3}$$

Numerator: $2 + j3 = \sqrt{13}\angle 56.31^\circ = 3.6056\angle 56.31^\circ$.
Denominator: $6 + j3 = \sqrt{45}\angle 26.565^\circ = 6.7082\angle 26.565^\circ$.

$$\dfrac{2 + j3}{6 + j3} = \dfrac{3.6056}{6.7082}\angle(56.31 - 26.565)^\circ = 0.5375\angle 29.745^\circ$$

$$\mathbf{I}_{4,2} = 2.0545 \cdot 0.5375 \angle (89.215 + 29.745)^\circ = 1.1043 \angle 118.96^\circ \text{ A}$$

**Total current by superposition:**
$$\mathbf{I}_4 = \mathbf{I}_{4,1} + \mathbf{I}_{4,2}$$

In rectangular:
- $\mathbf{I}_{4,1} = 1.9370\cos(-45.775^\circ) + j\, 1.9370\sin(-45.775^\circ) = 1.3500 - j1.3871$ A
- $\mathbf{I}_{4,2} = 1.1043\cos(118.96^\circ) + j\, 1.1043\sin(118.96^\circ) = -0.5340 + j0.9657$ A

Sum:
$$\mathbf{I}_4 = (1.3500 - 0.5340) + j(-1.3871 + 0.9657) = 0.8160 - j0.4214 \text{ A}$$

In polar:
$$\vert \mathbf{I}_4 \vert = \sqrt{0.8160^2 + 0.4214^2} = \sqrt{0.6659 + 0.1776} = \sqrt{0.8435} = 0.9184 \text{ A}$$
$$\angle \mathbf{I}_4 = \tan^{-1}(-0.4214 / 0.8160) = -27.30^\circ$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{I}_4 = 0.9184 \angle -27.30^\circ \text{ A}}$$

#### Part (b) Solution — Verification using Norton

**Norton current $\mathbf{I}_N$:** Short-circuit the $4 \, \Omega$ resistor (set $Z_L = 0$). The current through the short equals the Norton current.

With $Z_L$ shorted, $\mathbf{V}_1$ (through $Z_a$) and $\mathbf{V}_2$ (through $Z_b$) drive a common node. By Millman's theorem (a form of nodal analysis), the short-circuit current is:

$$\mathbf{I}_N = \mathbf{V}_1 Y_a + \mathbf{V}_2 Y_b = \mathbf{V}_1 / Z_a + \mathbf{V}_2 / Z_b$$

$$\mathbf{I}_N = \dfrac{20\angle 0^\circ}{2 + j3} + \dfrac{10\angle 90^\circ}{3 - j1}$$

First term: $\dfrac{20\angle 0^\circ}{\sqrt{13}\angle 56.31^\circ} = \dfrac{20}{3.6056}\angle -56.31^\circ = 5.5470\angle -56.31^\circ$ A
In rectangular: $5.5470\cos(-56.31^\circ) + j\, 5.5470\sin(-56.31^\circ) = 3.0772 - j4.6154$ A

Second term: $\dfrac{10\angle 90^\circ}{\sqrt{10}\angle -18.435^\circ} = \dfrac{10}{3.1623}\angle 108.435^\circ = 3.1623\angle 108.435^\circ$ A
In rectangular: $3.1623\cos(108.435^\circ) + j\, 3.1623\sin(108.435^\circ) = -0.9899 + j3.0000$ A

Sum:
$$\mathbf{I}_N = (3.0772 - 0.9899) + j(-4.6154 + 3.0000) = 2.0873 - j1.6154 \text{ A}$$

In polar: $\vert \mathbf{I}_N \vert = \sqrt{4.3568 + 2.6095} = \sqrt{6.9663} = 2.6394$ A, $\angle = \tan^{-1}(-1.6154/2.0873) = -37.74^\circ$.

$$\mathbf{I}_N = 2.6394\angle -37.74^\circ \text{ A}$$

**Norton impedance $Z_N$:** Deactivate both sources. $Z_N = Z_a \parallel Z_b$:
$$Z_N = \dfrac{Z_a Z_b}{Z_a + Z_b} = \dfrac{(2 + j3)(3 - j1)}{(2 + j3) + (3 - j1)} = \dfrac{6 - j2 + j9 + 3}{5 + j2} = \dfrac{9 + j7}{5 + j2}$$

$$= \dfrac{(9 + j7)(5 - j2)}{25 + 4} = \dfrac{45 - j18 + j35 + 14}{29} = \dfrac{59 + j17}{29} = 2.0345 + j0.5862 \, \Omega$$

**Verification: Current through $4 \, \Omega$ resistor using Norton**

Total impedance seen by Norton source:
$$Z_{total} = Z_N + 4 = (2.0345 + j0.5862) + 4 = 6.0345 + j0.5862 \, \Omega$$

In polar: $\vert Z_{total} \vert = \sqrt{36.415 + 0.3436} = \sqrt{36.759} = 6.0629$, $\angle = \tan^{-1}(0.5862/6.0345) = 5.547^\circ$.

$$\mathbf{I}_4 = \dfrac{\mathbf{I}_N}{Z_{total}} \cdot \text{(current through 4 Ω branch)} = \dfrac{2.6394\angle -37.74^\circ}{6.0629 \angle 5.547^\circ} = 0.4354\angle -43.29^\circ \text{ A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** The student has applied a current divider incorrectly. The Norton equivalent is in **parallel** with the load, not in series. The voltage across the parallel combination is $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I}_N \cdot (Z_N \parallel Z_L)$, not $\mathbf{I}_N \cdot (Z_N + Z_L)$. The **load current** is then $\mathbf{I}_L = \mathbf{V} / Z_L$. The calculation above used the correct parallel interpretation: $\mathbf{I}_L = \mathbf{I}_N \cdot \dfrac{Z_N}{Z_N + Z_L}$.
>
> $$\mathbf{I}_4 = 2.6394\angle -37.74^\circ \cdot \dfrac{2.0345 + j0.5862}{6.0345 + j0.5862}$$
> In polar: $\dfrac{\vert Z_N \vert}{\vert Z_N + Z_L \vert} = \dfrac{2.1171}{6.0629} = 0.3492$, $\angle = \tan^{-1}(0.5862/2.0345) - \tan^{-1}(0.5862/6.0345) = 16.08 - 5.55 = 10.53^\circ$.
>
> $$\mathbf{I}_4 = 2.6394 \cdot 0.3492 \angle (-37.74 + 10.53)^\circ = 0.9217 \angle -27.21^\circ \text{ A}$$
>
> This **matches the superposition result** within rounding error, confirming both methods. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to phasors first** — Half the marks are lost if you treat impedances as resistances. Always draw the phasor-domain circuit before any theorem application.
> 2. **Mixing up $j$ placement** — Inductor: $j\omega L$ on the **+j** axis; Capacitor: $-j/(\omega C)$ on the **−j** axis. Reverse placement flips the sign of the answer.
> 3. **Sign error in superposition** — When deactivating sources, the *impedances* remain in the circuit — only the **sources** are zeroed. Voltage sources become **short circuits**, current sources become **open circuits**.
> 4. **Forgetting the conjugate in maximum power** — Maximum power with a **complex load** requires $Z_L = Z_{Th}^{\,*}$, NOT $Z_L = Z_{Th}$. Using the same impedance gives zero current, not maximum power.
> 5. **Unit errors** — Phasor voltages and currents are complex. Their **magnitudes** carry units (V, A), but their **phases** are pure numbers in degrees or radians.
> 6. **Inverse phasor conversion skipped** — A phasor answer without the corresponding time-domain sinusoid is incomplete. Always provide $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for KTU Board Exam**

- [ ] **Phasor transform rule:** $V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi) \leftrightarrow V_m \angle \phi$. Use cosine reference unless stated otherwise.
- [ ] **Impedance substitutions:** $R \to R$, $L \to j\omega L$, $C \to 1/(j\omega C)$. The DC theorems work identically on these complex impedances.
- [ ] **Superposition** is the **only** theorem that works across multiple frequencies — apply it **frequency by frequency** for multi-frequency sources, then sum the *time-domain* responses.
- [ ] **Thevenin equivalent** requires two pieces: $\mathbf{V}_{OC}$ (open-circuit phasor voltage) and $Z_{Th}$ (impedance with all sources zeroed, looking into the terminals).
- [ ] **Norton equivalent** is the current-source dual: $\mathbf{I}_N = \mathbf{I}_{SC}$ and $Z_N = Z_{Th}$. Source transformation: $\mathbf{V}_{Th} = \mathbf{I}_N \cdot Z_{Th}$.
- [ ] **Maximum Power Transfer (MPT)** — variable complex load optimum: $Z_L = Z_{Th}^{\,*}$. Maximum power formula: $P_{max} = \vert \mathbf{V}_{Th} \vert^2 / (4 R_{Th})$. For purely resistive load, $R_L = R_{Th}$ **only** when $X_{Th} = 0$.
- [ ] **Reciprocity theorem** applies to **single-source, linear, bilateral, passive** networks. It says source-to-response ratio is invariant under source/responder swap.
- [ ] **Millman theorem** is the fastest path for multi-branch parallel networks: $\mathbf{V}_{AB} = (\sum \mathbf{V}_k Y_k) / (\sum Y_k)$.
- [ ] **Series-parallel reductions** in phasor form use the **same formulas** as DC, but with complex arithmetic. Always convert to polar before multiplying/dividing; always convert to rectangular before adding/subtracting.
- [ ] **Final answer format** — Always state the answer as a **phasor** AND its **time-domain sinusoidal equivalent**. Tag with units (V, A, W) and phase (degrees or radians).
- [ ] **Sanity check** — Verify that the imaginary part of $Z_{Th}$ is non-zero **only** if there are reactive elements in the source-side network. If your $Z_{Th}$ is purely real, the network has no reactive storage.
- [ ] **Source independence** — Superposition requires sources to be **independent** (not controlled by other circuit variables). Controlled sources stay ACTIVE during superposition and are deactivated only for Thevenin/Norton impedance computation.
- [ ] **Frequency dependence** — In AC, $Z_{Th}$ is a function of $\omega$. If the question specifies a frequency, compute at that frequency. If it says "design a load for broadband match," you need an LC network to synthesise $Z_{Th}^{\,*}(\omega)$ over a band.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circuit Analysis through Laplace Transform: Review of Laplace Transform and Inverse Laplace Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIT303 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circuit Analysis through Laplace Transform: Review of Laplace Transform and Inverse Laplace Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Laplace Transform

The **Laplace Transform** is a powerful integral transform that converts a time-domain function $f(t)$ into a complex frequency-domain (s-domain) function $F(s)$, where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is a complex variable. It is formally defined for $t \geq 0$ as:

$$L\{f(t)\} = F(s) = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In circuit analysis, the lower limit is taken as $0^-$ (just before switching) to capture initial conditions of capacitors and inductors, including any impulses at $t=0$.

The **Inverse Laplace Transform** recovers the time-domain function from its s-domain counterpart:

$$L^{-1}\{F(s)\} = f(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} F(s)\, e^{st}\, ds$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Audio Equalizer

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Imagine listening to a music track with many instruments playing simultaneously (this is your time-domain signal $f(t)$). A Laplace Transform is like an **audio equalizer** that separates the track into individual frequency bands. Instead of a complex mix, you now see clear sliders for bass, mid, and treble. **Algebraic manipulation in the s-domain is far easier than solving differential equations in the time domain**, just as adjusting individual equalizer sliders is easier than re-recording the song with new instruments. Finally, the **Inverse Laplace Transform** reassembles the modified frequency bands back into a complete time-domain signal.

## 1.3 Why Laplace Transforms are Essential in Circuit Analysis

| Aspect | Time Domain | s-Domain (Laplace) |
|---|---|---|
| **Inductor Voltage** | $v_L = L\,\dfrac{di}{dt}$ | $V_L(s) = sL\,I(s) - L\,i(0^-)$ |
| **Capacitor Current** | $i_C = C\,\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | $I_C(s) = sC\,V_C(s) - C\,v(0^-)$ |
| **Differential Equations** | Complex integro-differential | Simple algebraic equations |
| **Initial Conditions** | Must be applied after solving | Automatically included |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** Laplace transforms convert circuit analysis from solving differential equations to solving algebraic equations, with initial conditions built-in.

## 1.4 Concept of the s-Plane

The complex variable $s = \sigma + j\omega$ defines the **s-plane** (also called the complex frequency plane). The real part $\sigma$ represents exponential growth/decay, and the imaginary part $j\omega$ represents oscillation. The location of poles (roots of denominator) and zeros (roots of numerator) in the s-plane completely determines the nature of the time-domain response.

![Laplace Transform Workflow Overview - Mermaid Diagram in Section 4]
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Existence of the Laplace Transform

For the integral to converge, the function $f(t)$ must satisfy:

1. $f(t)$ is piecewise continuous on every finite interval in $[0, \infty)$.
2. $f(t)$ is of **exponential order**: $\vert f(t) \vert \leq M\,e^{\alpha t}$ for some constants $M > 0$ and $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$.

If both conditions hold, the transform exists for $\text{Re}(s) > \alpha$.

## 2.2 Logical Steps for Applying Laplace Transform in Circuits

- **Step 1 — Convert elements to s-domain:** Replace each resistor with $R$, inductor with $sL$ (plus a voltage source $Li(0^-)$ in series, or current source $i(0^-)/s$ in parallel), capacitor with $1/sC$ (plus a voltage source $v(0^-)/s$ in series, or current source $Cv(0^-)$ in parallel).
- **Step 2 — Apply KVL/KCL algebraically:** Use mesh or nodal analysis in s-domain.
- **Step 3 — Solve for the desired response:** Obtain $V(s)$ or $I(s)$ as a rational function.
- **Step 4 — Apply Inverse Laplace Transform:** Use partial fraction expansion to recover $v(t)$ or $i(t)$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam critical.** Memorize the table thoroughly.

| # | Property / Transform Pair | Mathematical Statement | s-Domain Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linearity** | $L\{a\,f(t) + b\,g(t)\}$ | $a\,F(s) + b\,G(s)$ |
| 2 | **Time Shifting** | $L\{f(t-a)\,u(t-a)\}$ | $e^{-as}\,F(s)$ |
| 3 | **Frequency Shifting** | $L\{e^{at}\,f(t)\}$ | $F(s-a)$ |
| 4 | **Time Scaling** | $L\{f(at)\}$ | $\dfrac{1}{a}\,F\!\left(\dfrac{s}{a}\right)$ |
| 5 | **First Derivative** | $L\!\left\{\dfrac{df}{dt}\right\}$ | $s\,F(s) - f(0^-)$ |
| 6 | **nth Derivative** | $L\!\left\{\dfrac{d^n f}{dt^n}\right\}$ | $s^n F(s) - \sum_{k=1}^{n} s^{n-k} f^{(k-1)}(0^-)$ |
| 7 | **Integration** | $L\!\left\{\displaystyle\int_0^t f(\tau)\,d\tau\right\}$ | $\dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ |
| 8 | **Convolution (Real)** | $L\{f(t) * g(t)\}$ | $F(s) \cdot G(s)$ |
| 9 | **Initial Value Theorem** | $\displaystyle\lim_{t \to 0^+} f(t)$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to \infty} s\,F(s)$ |
| 10 | **Final Value Theorem** | $\displaystyle\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t)$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to 0} s\,F(s)$ |
| 11 | **Unit Step** | $L\{u(t)\}$ | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ |
| 12 | **Unit Impulse** | $L\{\delta(t)\}$ | $1$ |
| 13 | **Exponential Decay** | $L\{e^{-at}\,u(t)\}$ | $\dfrac{1}{s+a}$ |
| 14 | **Sine Wave** | $L\{\sin(\omega t)\,u(t)\}$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ |
| 15 | **Cosine Wave** | $L\{\cos(\omega t)\,u(t)\}$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ |
| 16 | **Damped Sine** | $L\{e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)\,u(t)\}$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ |
| 17 | **Damped Cosine** | $L\{e^{-at}\cos(\omega t)\,u(t)\}$ | $\dfrac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ |
| 18 | **Ramp Function** | $L\{t\,u(t)\}$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ |
| 19 | **Power Function** | $L\{t^n\,u(t)\}$ | $\dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$ |
| 20 | **Complex Exponential** | $L\{e^{-at}\cos(\omega t) + j\sin(\omega t)\}$ | $\dfrac{1}{s+a-j\omega}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The **Final Value Theorem** is valid ONLY if all poles of $sF(s)$ lie in the **open left half-plane (LHP)**. If any pole is on the $j\omega$-axis or in the right half-plane (RHP), the theorem cannot be applied.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Control Systems:** Transfer functions $H(s) = Y(s)/X(s)$ characterize linear time-invariant (LTI) systems.
- **Signal Processing:** Filtering, modulation, and deconvolution rely on Laplace-domain techniques.
- **Power Systems:** Transient analysis of switching events, fault analysis.
- **Communication:** Stability analysis using pole locations in the s-plane.
- **VLSI/Embedded Design:** RC, RL, and RLC circuit response characterization.

## 2.5 Partial Fraction Expansion — The Backbone of Inverse Laplace

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Partial Fractions?** Most $F(s)$ functions in circuit analysis are rational: $F(s) = N(s)/D(s)$. To apply the inverse transform, we must split $F(s)$ into a sum of simple terms that match known transform pairs.

### Three Cases for Inverse Laplace via PFE:

**Case 1 — Real and Distinct Poles:**
$$F(s) = \frac{N(s)}{(s+p_1)(s+p_2)\cdots(s+p_n)} = \frac{K_1}{s+p_1} + \frac{K_2}{s+p_2} + \cdots + \frac{K_n}{s+p_n}$$

**Case 2 — Repeated (Repeated-Order) Poles:**
$$F(s) = \frac{N(s)}{(s+p)^n} = \frac{K_1}{s+p} + \frac{K_2}{(s+p)^2} + \cdots + \frac{K_n}{(s+p)^n}$$

**Case 3 — Complex Conjugate Poles:**
$$F(s) = \frac{N(s)}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2} = \frac{K(s+a) + M\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$$
which gives: $K e^{-at}\cos(\omega t) + M e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)$.

The **Heaviside Cover-Up Method** gives residues for distinct poles:
$$K_i = (s+p_i) \cdot F(s) \Big\vert_{s=-p_i}$$

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Linearity Property

We begin with $f(t) = a\,f_1(t) + b\,f_2(t)$ and apply the definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L\{a\,f_1(t) + b\,f_2(t)\} &= \int_{0^-}^{\infty} \big[a\,f_1(t) + b\,f_2(t)\big]\,e^{-st}\,dt \\
&= a\int_{0^-}^{\infty} f_1(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt + b\int_{0^-}^{\infty} f_2(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt \\
&= a\,F_1(s) + b\,F_2(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

> This confirms that the Laplace transform is a **linear operator** — fundamental for handling circuit superposition in the s-domain.

## 3.2 Derivation of the First-Derivative Property

Starting from the definition and integrating by parts with $u = f(t)$, $dv = e^{-st}dt$, $du = f'(t)dt$, $v = -e^{-st}/s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L\{f'(t)\} &= \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f'(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt \\
&= \Big[f(t)\,e^{-st}\Big]_{0^-}^{\infty} - \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t)\left(-s\,e^{-st}\right)dt \\
&= \Big[0 - f(0^-)\Big] + s\int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt \\
&= s\,F(s) - f(0^-)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why It Matters:** This is the **single most important property for circuits**, since KVL and KCL inherently involve derivatives of inductor currents and capacitor voltages.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Integration Property

Let $g(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0^-}^{t} f(\tau)\,d\tau$. Then $g'(t) = f(t)$ and $g(0^-) = 0$. Apply the derivative property to $g(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L\{g'(t)\} &= s\,G(s) - g(0^-) \\
F(s) &= s\,G(s) - 0 \\
G(s) &= \frac{F(s)}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Frequency Shifting Property

Apply the definition to $e^{at}f(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L\{e^{at}f(t)\} &= \int_{0^-}^{\infty} e^{at}\,f(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt \\
&= \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t)\,e^{-(s-a)t}\,dt \\
&= F(s-a)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Multiplication by $e^{at}$ in the time domain corresponds to a **horizontal shift** of $F(s)$ by $a$ in the s-plane. This is why a damped oscillation $e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)$ corresponds to poles shifted to $\sigma = -a$.

## 3.5 Derivation of the Initial Value Theorem

From the derivative property: $L\{f'(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$. Taking $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to \infty}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{s \to \infty} L\{f'(t)\} &= \lim_{s \to \infty}\big[sF(s) - f(0^-)\big] \\
\lim_{s \to \infty}\int_{0^-}^{\infty} f'(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt &= \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s) - f(0^-)
\end{aligned}
$$

The integral on the left vanishes as $s \to \infty$ (because $e^{-st}$ suppresses all finite-time behavior). Hence:

$$f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s)$$

## 3.6 Derivation of the Final Value Theorem

Starting again with the derivative property:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{s \to 0}\big[sF(s) - f(0^-)\big] &= \lim_{s \to 0}\int_{0^-}^{\infty} f'(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt \\
\lim_{s \to 0} sF(s) - f(0^-) &= \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f'(t)\,dt \\
\lim_{s \to 0} sF(s) &= f(0^-) + \big[f(\infty) - f(0^-)\big] = f(\infty)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\displaystyle\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$, valid only if the limit exists (no RHP poles, no $j\omega$-axis poles except at origin).

## 3.7 Worked Example — Full Inverse Laplace via PFE

**Problem:** Find $f(t)$ for $\displaystyle F(s) = \frac{s+3}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$.

**Step 1 — Factor the denominator:**
$$s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$$

**Step 2 — Set up partial fractions:**
$$F(s) = \frac{K_1}{s+1} + \frac{K_2}{s+2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Cover-Up Method:**

For $K_1$ (multiply by $(s+1)$ and set $s=-1$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 &= \left.\frac{s+3}{s+2}\right\vert_{s=-1} = \frac{-1+3}{-1+2} = \frac{2}{1} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

For $K_2$ (multiply by $(s+2)$ and set $s=-2$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_2 &= \left.\frac{s+3}{s+1}\right\vert_{s=-2} = \frac{-2+3}{-2+1} = \frac{1}{-1} = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the decomposed form:**
$$F(s) = \frac{2}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the inverse transform using $L^{-1}\{1/(s+a)\} = e^{-at}u(t)$:**
$$f(t) = \big[2e^{-t} - e^{-2t}\big]u(t)$$

> **Verification using initial and final value theorems:**
> - Initial: $f(0^+) = 2 - 1 = 1$. Check: $\lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s) = \lim_{s \to \infty}\frac{s^2+3s}{s^2+3s+2} = 1$. ✓
> - Final: $f(\infty) = 0$. Check: $\lim_{s \to 0} sF(s) = 0$. ✓

## 3.8 Worked Example — Complex Conjugate Poles

**Problem:** Find $f(t)$ for $\displaystyle F(s) = \frac{s+4}{s^2 + 4s + 13}$.

**Step 1 — Complete the square in the denominator:**
$$s^2 + 4s + 13 = (s+2)^2 + 9 = (s+2)^2 + 3^2$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite the numerator in the shifted form:**
$$s + 4 = (s + 2) + 2$$

**Step 3 — Express as sum of standard forms:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F(s) &= \frac{(s+2)}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2} + \frac{2}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2} \\
&= \frac{(s+2)}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2} + \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply inverse transforms:**

Using $L^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}\right\} = e^{-at}\cos(\omega t)$ and $L^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}\right\} = e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)$:

$$f(t) = \left[e^{-2t}\cos(3t) + \frac{2}{3}e^{-2t}\sin(3t)\right]u(t)$$

## 3.9 Worked Example — Repeated Poles

**Problem:** Find $f(t)$ for $\displaystyle F(s) = \frac{1}{s^2(s+1)}$.

**Step 1 — Set up partial fractions for repeated pole at $s=0$:**

$$F(s) = \frac{K_1}{s} + \frac{K_2}{s^2} + \frac{K_3}{s+1}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $K_2$ (cover-up for $1/s^2$):**
$$K_2 = \left.\frac{1}{s+1}\right\vert_{s=0} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $K_3$ (cover-up for $s+1$):**
$$K_3 = \left.\frac{1}{s^2}\right\vert_{s=-1} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $K_1$ (using a common point, e.g., $s=1$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left.\frac{1}{s^2(s+1)}\right\vert_{s=1} &= \frac{K_1}{1} + \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{2} \\
\frac{1}{2} &= K_1 + 1 + \frac{1}{2} \\
K_1 &= -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Inverse transform:**

$$f(t) = \left[-1 + t + e^{-t}\right]u(t)$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Laplace Transform Workflow in Circuit Analysis

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Time Domain Circuit] --> B[Identify Initial Conditions]
    B --> C[Convert Elements to s-Domain]
    C --> D[Apply KVL or KCL Algebraically]
    D --> E[Solve for Desired F of s]
    E --> F{Is F of s Rational?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Perform Partial Fraction Expansion]
    F -->|No| H[Use Convolution or Direct Inversion]
    G --> I[Match Terms with Standard Table]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply Inverse Laplace Transform]
    J --> K[Final Time Domain Response f of t]
    K --> L[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Inverse Laplace via Partial Fractions

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input F of s] --> B{Proper Rational Function?}
    B -->|No| C[Perform Long Division]
    B -->|Yes| D[Factor Denominator D of s]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Identify Pole Types}
    E -->|Distinct Real| F[Cover Up Method K1 K2 Kn]
    E -->|Repeated| G[Repeated Pole Formula K1 to Km]
    E -->|Complex Conjugate| H[Complete Square and Decompose]
    F --> I[Sum of Simple Fractions]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Match with Standard Laplace Table]
    J --> K[Final f of t with Exponential and Sinusoidal Terms]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — s-Plane Pole-Zero Visualization (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[s Plane Representation] --> B[Real Axis sigma]
    A --> C[Imaginary Axis j omega]
    B --> D[Left Half Plane Stable Region]
    B --> E[Origin j omega Axis]
    B --> F[Right Half Plane Unstable Region]
    D --> G[Exponential Decay Terms e to power minus at]
    F --> H[Exponential Growth Terms e to power plus at]
    E --> I[Pure Oscillation sin and cos]
    G --> J[Damped Oscillation]
    H --> K[Growing Oscillation]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — s-Domain Element Models

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Resistor R] --> A1[Impedance is R]
    B[Inductor L] --> B1[Impedance sL in series with Voltage Source Li zero minus]
    B --> B2[OR Impedance sL in parallel with Current Source i zero minus over s]
    C[Capacitor C] --> C1[Impedance one over sC in series with Voltage Source v zero minus over s]
    C --> C2[OR Impedance one over sC in parallel with Current Source Cv zero minus]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Tip for KTU:** When converting elements, always draw both the **s-domain impedance** AND the **initial condition source** on your circuit diagram. A common mistake is forgetting the source representation, which leads to wrong answers even with correct algebra.
