| $\omega = \Omega_i$ (resonance) | Depends on $z_j$ | Pole vector becomes purely imaginary (perpendicular to real axis) | Resonance peak if $\sigma_i$ small | Sharp phase transition of $\approx 90°$ per pole |
| $\omega \to \infty$ | All vectors $\approx \omega$ along positive imaginary axis | All vectors $\approx \omega$ along positive imaginary axis | $\to \vert K \vert \cdot \omega^{m-n}$ | $\to 90°(m - n)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **end-to-end process** of computing amplitude and phase response from a given pole-zero plot, in line with the KTU examiner's preferred method of solution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Pole-Zero Plot of H s] --> B[Identify all poles p_i and zeros z_j with their real and imaginary parts]
    B --> C[Select a test frequency omega on the j omega axis]
    C --> D[Draw vector from each zero z_j to the test point j omega]
    C --> E[Draw vector from each pole p_i to the test point j omega]
    D --> F[Compute zero vector magnitude N_j and angle phi_j]
    E --> G[Compute pole vector magnitude M_i and angle theta_i]
    F --> H[Apply magnitude formula: H of j omega equals K times product of N_j over product of M_i]
    G --> H
    F --> I[Apply phase formula: angle H of j omega equals angle K plus sum phi_j minus sum theta_i]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Record magnitude value in dB or linear scale at current omega]
    I --> K[Record phase value in degrees or radians at current omega]
    J --> L[Sweep omega from zero to infinity to trace full response]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Output: Amplitude and Phase plots versus omega]
```

### 4.2 Pole-Zero Layout and Vector Construction (Schematic View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP_Plane["s-PLANE"]
        A1[Z j omega — Test Point on Imaginary Axis] --- A2[Vector from zero z_1 to j omega — length N_1 angle phi_1]
        A3[Vector from pole p_1 to j omega — length M_1 angle theta_1] --- A4[Vector from pole p_2 to j omega — length M_2 angle theta_2]
        B1[Zero z_1 located at sigma_z + j Omega_z] --- A2
        B2[Pole p_1 located at sigma_p1 + j Omega_p1] --- A3
        B3[Pole p_2 located at sigma_p2 + j Omega_p2] --- A4
    end
    A2 --> R1[H j omega magnitude = K times N_1 over M_1 times M_2]
    A3 --> R1
    A4 --> R1
    A2 --> R2[H j omega phase = phi_1 minus theta_1 minus theta_2]
    A3 --> R2
    A4 --> R2
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Identifying Response Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Read Pole-Zero Plot] --> S1{Is there a pole on the j omega axis?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[System is marginally stable: magnitude goes to infinity at that frequency]
    S1 -- No --> S3{Is there a complex conjugate pole pair near the j omega axis?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Resonance peak in amplitude response: band-pass or resonant low-pass behaviour]
    S3 -- No --> S5{Count number of poles minus zeros: m minus n}
    S5 -- Greater than zero --> S6[High-pass behaviour: magnitude rises with omega, phase ends positive]
    S5 -- Equal to zero --> S7[All-pass or flat behaviour: magnitude flat, phase varies]
    S5 -- Less than zero --> S8[Low-pass behaviour: magnitude falls with omega, phase ends negative]
    S2 --> S9[Annotate Phase Plot: identify 90 degree transitions at each pole]
    S4 --> S9
    S6 --> S9
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This decision tree is a high-yield visual aid for the KTU exam. By simply counting the difference $m - n$ between the number of finite zeros and finite poles, the student can immediately predict the asymptotic slope of the magnitude curve (in dB/decade) and the asymptotic phase limit at high frequency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1.** What is meant by the *frequency response* of a network? Explain how it is obtained from the pole–zero plot of its transfer function. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The frequency response of a network is the variation of the magnitude and phase of its transfer function as a function of real frequency $\omega$, obtained by substituting $s = j\omega$ into the transfer function $H(s)$.

From the pole–zero plot, the frequency response is determined **geometrically** as follows. For each test frequency $j\omega$ on the imaginary axis, a vector is drawn from every pole $p_i$ and every zero $z_j$ to the point $j\omega$. The magnitude of the response is:

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \vert K \vert \cdot \frac{\prod_{j} N_{j}}{\prod_{i} M_{i}}
$$

where $N_j$ and $M_i$ are the lengths of the zero and pole vectors, respectively. The phase is the sum of zero-vector angles minus the sum of pole-vector angles:

$$
\angle H(j\omega) = \angle K + \sum_{j} \phi_j - \sum_{i} \theta_i
$$

Sweeping $\omega$ from $0$ to $\infty$ yields the full amplitude and phase response curves.

---

**Q2.** A network has a single pole at $s = -3$ and a single zero at $s = -1$, with $K = 1$. Compute the magnitude of $H(j\omega)$ at $\omega = 0$ and at $\omega \to \infty$. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The transfer function is $H(s) = \dfrac{s + 1}{s + 3}$.

**At $\omega = 0$:**

The vector from the zero $z_1 = -1$ to $j0$ has length $N_1 = \vert 0 - (-1) \vert = 1$.

The vector from the pole $p_1 = -3$ to $j0$ has length $M_1 = \vert 0 - (-3) \vert = 3$.

$$
\vert H(j0) \vert = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

In dB: $20 \log_{10}(0.333) = -9.54$ dB.

**At $\omega \to \infty$:**

As $\omega$ becomes very large, both vectors have length $\approx \omega$, so the ratio approaches $1$. Hence:

$$
\lim_{\omega \to \infty} \vert H(j\omega) \vert = 1 = 0 \text{ dB}
$$

This indicates a **high-pass-like** rising response (from $-9.54$ dB up to $0$ dB).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Evaluation, KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** The transfer function of a linear network is:

$$
H(s) = \frac{s + 1}{(s + 2)(s + 5)}
$$

Draw the pole–zero plot, compute the magnitude and phase of $H(j\omega)$ at $\omega = 0, 1, 3, 10$ rad/s using the geometric method, and tabulate your results. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Using the tabulated values from part (a), sketch the amplitude response $|H(j\omega)|$ (in dB) and the phase response $\angle H(j\omega)$ (in degrees) versus $\omega$. State the type of filter behaviour exhibited by this network. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify poles and zeros:**

- Zero: $z_1 = -1$
- Poles: $p_1 = -2$, $p_2 = -5$
- Gain: $K = 1$

**Step 2 — Vector formulas:**

$$
N_1(\omega) = \sqrt{1 + \omega^{2}}, \quad \phi_1 = \tan^{-1}(\omega)
$$

$$
M_1(\omega) = \sqrt{4 + \omega^{2}}, \quad \theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)
$$

$$
M_2(\omega) = \sqrt{25 + \omega^{2}}, \quad \theta_2 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{5}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude and phase equations:**

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\sqrt{1 + \omega^{2}}}{\sqrt{(4 + \omega^{2})(25 + \omega^{2})}}
$$

$$
\angle H(j\omega) = \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{5}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation table:**

| $\omega$ | $N_1$ | $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ | $20\log_{10}\vert H \vert$ (dB) | $\phi_1$ (deg) | $\theta_1$ (deg) | $\theta_2$ (deg) | $\angle H(j\omega)$ (deg) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0  | 1.000 | 2.000 | 5.000 | 0.1000 | -20.00 | 0.00  | 0.00  | 0.00  | 0.00  |
| 1  | 1.414 | 2.236 | 5.099 | 0.1240 | -18.13 | 45.00 | 26.57 | 11.31 | 7.12  |
| 3  | 3.162 | 3.606 | 5.831 | 0.1504 | -16.45 | 71.57 | 56.31 | 30.96 | -15.70 |
| 10 | 10.05 | 10.20 | 11.18 | 0.0880 | -21.11 | 84.29 | 78.69 | 63.43 | -57.83 |

**Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Step 1 — Sketch the amplitude response in dB:**

- At $\omega = 0$: $-20$ dB.
- Rises gradually to a **peak of $\approx -16.45$ dB at $\omega = 3$ rad/s**.
- Falls asymptotically toward $-\infty$ dB at high $\omega$ with slope $-40$ dB/decade (since there are 2 more poles than zeros).

**Step 2 — Sketch the phase response:**

- Starts at $0°$ for $\omega = 0$.
- Has a small positive excursion (peak $\approx +7°$ near $\omega = 1$ rad/s).
- Rolls off monotonically to $-90°$ as $\omega \to \infty$ (asymptotic phase = $90°(m - n) = 90°(1 - 2) = -90°$).

**Step 3 — Filter type:**

Since the magnitude peaks at a finite non-zero frequency and then rolls off, this is a **low-pass filter with mild resonant peaking** — more precisely, a **second-order low-pass filter** with a finite zero introducing a slight high-frequency emphasis in the passband.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** A network has the pole–zero plot shown below:
- Zeros at $s = 0$ and $s = -4$
- Poles at $s = -1$ and $s = -2$
- Gain $K = 4$

Determine the transfer function $H(s)$ and compute the magnitude (in dB) and phase (in degrees) of $H(j\omega)$ at $\omega = 0, 1, 2, 4, 10$ rad/s. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Comment on the asymptotic behaviour of the magnitude and phase plots as $\omega \to 0$ and $\omega \to \infty$. Identify the filter type and justify your answer. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Step 1 — Construct the transfer function:**

$$
H(s) = 4 \cdot \frac{s(s + 4)}{(s + 1)(s + 2)}
$$

**Step 2 — Vector formulas:**

For the zero at $z_1 = 0$:

$$
N_1(\omega) = \omega, \quad \phi_1(\omega) = 90°
$$

For the zero at $z_2 = -4$:

$$
N_2(\omega) = \sqrt{16 + \omega^{2}}, \quad \phi_2(\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{4}\right)
$$

For the pole at $p_1 = -1$:

$$
M_1(\omega) = \sqrt{1 + \omega^{2}}, \quad \theta_1(\omega) = \tan^{-1}(\omega)
$$

For the pole at $p_2 = -2$:

$$
M_2(\omega) = \sqrt{4 + \omega^{2}}, \quad \theta_2(\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude and phase equations:**

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = 4 \cdot \frac{\omega \cdot \sqrt{16 + \omega^{2}}}{\sqrt{(1 + \omega^{2})(4 + \omega^{2})}}
$$

$$
\angle H(j\omega) = 90° + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{4}\right) - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation table:**

| $\omega$ | $N_1$ | $N_2$ | $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $\vert H \vert$ | dB | $\phi_1$ | $\phi_2$ | $\theta_1$ | $\theta_2$ | Phase (deg) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0  | 0.000 | 4.000 | 1.000 | 2.000 | 0.000 | $-\infty$ | 90  | 0    | 0    | 0    | +90  |
| 1  | 1.000 | 4.123 | 1.414 | 2.236 | 5.832 | 15.32     | 90  | 14.04 | 45.00 | 26.57 | 32.47 |
| 2  | 2.000 | 4.472 | 2.236 | 2.828 | 5.657 | 15.05     | 90  | 26.57 | 63.43 | 45.00 | 8.14  |
| 4  | 4.000 | 5.657 | 4.123 | 4.472 | 4.902 | 13.81     | 90  | 45.00 | 75.96 | 63.43 | -4.39 |
| 10 | 10.00 | 10.77 | 10.05 | 10.20 | 4.208 | 12.48     | 90  | 68.20 | 84.29 | 78.69 | -4.78 |

**Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**As $\omega \to 0$:** The zero at the origin forces $\vert H(j\omega) \vert \to 0$ (magnitude goes to $-\infty$ dB), and the phase approaches $+90°$ because the $j\omega$ factor contributes a $+90°$ phase lead.

**As $\omega \to \infty$:** All vectors are of length $\approx \omega$, so:

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert \to 4 \cdot \frac{\omega^{2}}{\omega^{2}} = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 20\log_{10}(4) = 12.04 \text{ dB}
$$

The phase asymptotically approaches $90°(m - n) = 90°(2 - 2) = 0°$.

**Filter type:** Since the magnitude **rises** from $-\infty$ dB at low frequencies to a flat asymptote of $12.04$ dB at high frequencies, this is a **High-Pass Filter**. The network blocks DC completely (zero at origin) and passes high frequencies with a gain of $4$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $K$ factor:** Many students compute only the product ratio and forget that $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ includes $\vert K \vert$ and $\angle H(j\omega)$ includes $\angle K$. This silently loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Sign of the real part in the angle formula:** The vector is *from pole/zero to* $j\omega$, so the real part of the vector is $-\sigma$. Writing $\tan^{-1}(\omega/\sigma)$ instead of $\tan^{-1}(\omega / (-\sigma))$ introduces a 180° phase error and is a frequent valuation trap (lose 1–2 marks).
> 3. **Forgetting the $90°$ phase shift of the $j\omega$ factor:** A zero at the origin contributes exactly $+90°$ to the phase, not $0°$. Skipping this is a common 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Asymptotic slope misuse:** The high-frequency slope is $20(m - n)$ dB/decade. Counting $m - n$ incorrectly (e.g., writing $n - m$) is a recurring 1-mark error.
> 5. **No units on $\omega$:** Always quote $\omega$ in **rad/s**. The amplitude in dB has units of **dB**, and the phase in **degrees** — be consistent throughout.
> 6. **Skipping the geometric sketch:** A proper KTU 2024 answer includes the pole–zero plot *and* the drawn vectors at each test point. Skipping the visual loses 1–2 marks even if the numerical table is correct.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Frequency response is $H(j\omega) = H(s)\big\vert_{s = j\omega}$** — this is the bridge between the pole–zero plot and the amplitude/phase curves.
- **Magnitude formula:** $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \vert K \vert \cdot \dfrac{\prod_{j} N_{j}}{\prod_{i} M_{i}}$, where $N_j, M_i$ are **vector lengths** from zeros and poles to the test point $j\omega$.
- **Phase formula:** $\angle H(j\omega) = \angle K + \sum \phi_j - \sum \theta_i$, where $\phi_j, \theta_i$ are **vector angles** measured from the real axis.
- **Vector length from a point $a = \sigma + j\Omega$ to $j\omega$:** $L = \sqrt{\sigma^{2} + (\omega - \Omega)^{2}}$.
- **Vector angle:** $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega - \Omega}{-\sigma}\right)$ — note the **negative** sign in the denominator's real-part term.
- **Asymptotic magnitude at $\omega \to \infty$:** $\vert H(j\omega) \vert \to \vert K \vert \cdot \omega^{m-n}$ with dB slope $20(m - n)$ dB/decade.
- **Asymptotic phase at $\omega \to \infty$:** $\angle H(j\omega) \to 90°(m - n)$.
- **Asymptotic magnitude at $\omega = 0$:** $\vert H(j0) \vert = \vert K \vert \cdot \dfrac{\prod \vert z_j \vert}{\prod \vert p_i \vert}$ (if no pole/zero at origin).
- **Zero at origin** contributes a factor of $j\omega$, meaning the network **blocks DC** and has a $+90°$ phase lead at low frequencies — a hallmark of high-pass behaviour.
- **Pole at origin** contributes a factor of $1/(j\omega)$ with $-90°$ phase lag — typical of integrator / low-pass behaviour.
- **Complex conjugate pole pair near the imaginary axis** creates a **resonance peak** in amplitude and a sharp phase transition of approximately $180°$ across the resonance.
- **Filter-type identification rule:** If $m - n > 0$, the network is high-pass tending; if $m - n < 0$, it is low-pass tending; if $m - n = 0$, the magnitude asymptote is flat (all-pass-like).
- **Always convert to dB** ($20\log_{10}\vert H \vert$) when sketching the amplitude response, and **always use degrees** when sketching the phase response — these are KTU board conventions.
- **Geometric sketch is mandatory** in KTU answers: draw the pole–zero plot, mark the test points, and show the vectors. This earns full methodology marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIT303 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stability Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A linear time-invariant (LTI) system is said to be **stable** if and only if every bounded input applied to the system produces a **bounded output** (BIBO – Bounded Input Bounded Output stability). Mathematically, the necessary and sufficient condition for stability is that **all roots of the characteristic equation** (poles of the closed-loop transfer function) must lie strictly in the **left-half of the complex s-plane** (LHP), i.e., have **negative real parts**.

In the s-domain, a system is represented by a transfer function:

$$
H(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)}
$$

where $D(s) = 0$ is the **characteristic equation**. Stability analysis determines whether the roots of $D(s)$ satisfy:

$$
\Re(s_i) < 0, \quad \forall \text{ roots } s_i
$$

## 1.2 Types of Stability

| Stability Type | Pole Location | System Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Absolutely Stable** | All poles in strict LHP | Output decays to zero |
| **Marginally Stable** | Simple poles on jω-axis, rest in LHP | Sustained oscillations |
| **Unstable** | At least one pole in RHP or repeated poles on jω-axis | Output grows without bound |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Wobbly Table:**
> Imagine a table with four legs placed on a floor. If you push the table slightly, three scenarios can occur:
> 1. **Stable** — The table returns to its original position. The "push" dies down. *(Poles in LHP)*
> 2. **Marginally Stable** — The table slides back and forth forever without stopping. *(Pure imaginary poles)*
> 3. **Unstable** — The table topples over and never recovers. *(Poles in RHP)*
>
> In control systems, the "push" is the input, the table's response is the output, and the "leg positions" are determined by the system's characteristic equation roots.

## 1.4 Why Stability Criteria Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> - **Safety:** Unstable systems in aircraft, power grids, or chemical plants can cause catastrophic failures.
> - **Performance:** Even a stable system may be practically unusable if poorly damped.
> - **Design:** Stability criteria allow engineers to determine gain ranges, compensator parameters, and feedback loop configurations **before** building hardware.
> - **KTU Emphasis:** This topic carries significant weight in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination), typically appearing as a **14-mark analytical question**.

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Stability Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Routh-Hurwitz Criterion** — Algebraic method (no need to find roots).
> 2. **Nyquist Criterion** — Frequency-domain graphical method.
> 3. **Bode Plot Criterion** — Logarithmic frequency-response method with Gain/Phase Margins.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Characteristic Equation

For a closed-loop system with forward transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback $H(s)$:

$$
\text{Characteristic Equation: } 1 + G(s)H(s) = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The poles of the closed-loop transfer function are the roots of $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$. Stability is decided by the **location** of these roots in the s-plane.

## 2.2 Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion

The Routh-Hurwitz criterion is an **algebraic procedure** that determines the number of poles in the right-half plane (RHP) without explicitly solving the characteristic equation.

### 2.2.1 Construction of the Routh Array

Given characteristic polynomial:

$$
a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} s + a_n = 0
$$

**Routh Array Format:**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 | ... |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s^n$ | $a_0$ | $a_2$ | $a_4$ | ... |
| $s^{n-1}$ | $a_1$ | $a_3$ | $a_5$ | ... |
| $s^{n-2}$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | $b_3$ | ... |
| $s^{n-3}$ | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | ... | |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | | | |
| $s^0$ | $a_n$ | | | |

where:

$$
b_1 = \frac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}, \quad b_2 = \frac{a_1 a_4 - a_0 a_5}{a_1}, \quad b_3 = \frac{a_1 a_6 - a_0 a_7}{a_1}
$$

$$
c_1 = \frac{b_1 a_3 - a_1 b_2}{b_1}, \quad c_2 = \frac{b_1 a_5 - a_1 b_3}{b_1}
$$

### 2.2.2 Routh-Hurwitz Stability Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Routh Stability Conditions:**
> 1. All coefficients of the characteristic polynomial must be **real, non-zero, and have the same sign** (necessary condition).
> 2. All elements in the **first column** of the Routh array must have the **same sign** (sufficient condition).
> 3. The **number of sign changes** in the first column = **number of roots in the RHP**.

### 2.2.3 Special Cases in Routh Array

- **Case 1: Zero in first column, rest of row non-zero** → Replace zero with a small positive number $\varepsilon$ and continue.
- **Case 2: Entire row of zeros** → Indicates symmetric roots about origin. Form auxiliary polynomial from the row above, differentiate it, and use its coefficients to replace the zero row.

## 2.3 Hurwitz Criterion (Alternative Form)

For polynomial $a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n = 0$, the necessary and sufficient conditions for all roots to have negative real parts:

$$
\Delta_1 = a_1 > 0
$$

$$
\Delta_2 = \begin{vmatrix} a_1 & a_0 \\ a_3 & a_2 \end{vmatrix} > 0
$$

$$
\Delta_3 = \begin{vmatrix} a_1 & a_0 & 0 \\ a_3 & a_2 & a_1 \\ a_5 & a_4 & a_3 \end{vmatrix} > 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> All leading principal minors of the **Hurwitz matrix** must be positive.

## 2.4 Nyquist Stability Criterion

The Nyquist criterion uses the open-loop frequency response $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ plotted in the complex plane.

$$
N = Z - P
$$

where:
- $N$ = Number of **clockwise encirclements** of the critical point $(-1, j0)$
- $Z$ = Number of **zeros** of $1 + GH$ in the RHP (closed-loop unstable poles)
- $P$ = Number of **open-loop poles** of $GH$ in the RHP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For closed-loop stability, we need $Z = 0$, i.e., $N = -P$ (anticlockwise encirclements equal to number of RHP open-loop poles).

## 2.5 Bode Plot Stability — Gain & Phase Margins

- **Gain Crossover Frequency** ($\omega_{gc}$): Frequency at which $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = 1$ (i.e., 0 dB)
- **Phase Crossover Frequency** ($\omega_{pc}$): Frequency at which $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180°$

$$
\text{Gain Margin } (GM) = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}
$$

In decibels:

$$
GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} \right)
$$

$$
\text{Phase Margin } (PM) = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability in Bode Sense:**
> - $GM > 1$ (or $GM_{dB} > 0$) and $PM > 0°$ → **Stable system**
> - $GM < 1$ and $PM < 0°$ → **Unstable system**
> - Typical design targets: $GM \geq 6$ dB and $PM$ between $30°$ and $60°$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Condition for Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BIBO Stability | $\Re(s_i) < 0$ | All poles in LHP |
| 2 | Characteristic Eq. | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Roots determine stability |
| 3 | Routh 1st Col. | All same sign | No RHP poles |
| 4 | Sign changes | Count in 1st column | = Number of RHP poles |
| 5 | Nyquist | $N = Z - P$ | $Z = 0 \Rightarrow N = -P$ |
| 6 | Gain Margin | $GM = 1 \slash \vert GH(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | $GM > 1$ stable |
| 7 | Phase Margin | $PM = 180° + \angle GH(j\omega_{gc})$ | $PM > 0°$ stable |
| 8 | Hurwitz Determinant | $\Delta_i > 0, \forall i$ | All LHP poles |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aerospace:** Autopilot stability — Nyquist/Bode ensure aircraft pitch control remains stable across flight envelope.
> - **Power Systems:** Generator voltage regulators — Routh-Hurwitz determines safe gain limits.
> - **Electronics:** Oscillator design intentionally places poles on jω-axis (Barkhausen criterion).
> - **Biomedical:** Pacemaker feedback loops require rigorous BIBO stability to avoid dangerous heart-rate oscillations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Routh-Hurwitz Stability Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Determine the stability of a system whose characteristic equation is:
> $$s^4 + 2s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 5 = 0$$
> Find the number of roots in the right-half s-plane.

### Step 1: Verify Necessary Conditions

All coefficients are real, non-zero, and positive: $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ ✓
> Necessary condition satisfied — proceed to construct Routh array.

### Step 2: Initialize First Two Rows

For $s^4 + 2s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 5$, coefficients are:
$$a_0 = 1,\ a_1 = 2,\ a_2 = 3,\ a_3 = 4,\ a_4 = 5$$

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^4 & 1 & 3 & 5 \\
s^3 & 2 & 4 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the $s^2$ Row

$$
b_1 = \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(4)}{2} = \frac{6 - 4}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1
$$

$$
b_2 = \frac{(2)(5) - (1)(0)}{2} = \frac{10 - 0}{2} = 5
$$

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^4 & 1 & 3 & 5 \\
s^3 & 2 & 4 & 0 \\
s^2 & 1 & 5 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the $s^1$ Row

$$
c_1 = \frac{(1)(4) - (2)(5)}{1} = \frac{4 - 10}{1} = -6
$$

$$
c_2 = \frac{(1)(0) - (2)(0)}{1} = 0
$$

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^4 & 1 & 3 & 5 \\
s^3 & 2 & 4 & 0 \\
s^2 & 1 & 5 & 0 \\
s^1 & -6 & 0 & 0 \\
s^0 & 5 & 0 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

(For the $s^0$ row, we take the last coefficient directly: $5$)

### Step 5: Examine the First Column

First column: $\{1, 2, 1, -6, 5\}$

Sign sequence: $+\rightarrow+\rightarrow+\rightarrow-\rightarrow+$

### Step 6: Count Sign Changes

1. $+ \rightarrow -$ (at $s^1$ row) → **1st sign change**
2. $- \rightarrow +$ (at $s^0$ row) → **2nd sign change**

### Step 7: Conclude

$$
\boxed{\text{Number of RHP poles} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{System is UNSTABLE}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Finding Range of K for Stability (Routh-Hurwitz)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For a unity feedback system with $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$, find the range of $K$ for closed-loop stability.

### Step 1: Form the Characteristic Equation

$$
1 + G(s) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0
$$

$$
s(s+2)(s+4) + K = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
(s^2 + 2s)(s+4) + K = 0
$$

$$
s^3 + 4s^2 + 2s^2 + 8s + K = 0
$$

$$
s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0
$$

### Step 2: Construct Routh Array

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 8 \\
s^2 & 6 & K \\
s^1 & b_1 & 0 \\
s^0 & c_1 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

### Step 3: Compute $b_1$

$$
b_1 = \frac{(6)(8) - (1)(K)}{6} = \frac{48 - K}{6}
$$

### Step 4: Compute $c_1$

$$
c_1 = \frac{(b_1)(K) - (6)(0)}{b_1} = K
$$

### Step 5: Apply Stability Conditions

For stability, **all first-column elements must be positive**:

- $1 > 0$ ✓
- $6 > 0$ ✓
- $b_1 = \frac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K < 48$
- $c_1 = K > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K > 0$

### Step 6: Final Answer

$$
\boxed{0 < K < 48 \quad \text{(System is stable)}}
$$

At $K = 48$, the system is **marginally stable** (sustained oscillations at $\omega = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2}$ rad/s).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Hurwitz Determinant Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Apply Hurwitz criterion to: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6 = 0$.

Coefficients: $a_0 = 1, a_1 = 6, a_2 = 11, a_3 = 6$

**Hurwitz Matrix:**

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 & a_0 & 0 \\ a_3 & a_2 & a_1 \\ 0 & 0 & a_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 1 & 0 \\ 6 & 11 & 6 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Leading Principal Minors:**

$$
\Delta_1 = \vert 6 \vert = 6 > 0 \;\checkmark
$$

$$
\Delta_2 = \begin{vmatrix} 6 & 1 \\ 6 & 11 \end{vmatrix} = (6)(11) - (1)(6) = 66 - 6 = 60 > 0 \;\checkmark
$$

$$
\Delta_3 = \begin{vmatrix} 6 & 1 & 0 \\ 6 & 11 & 6 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 \end{vmatrix} = 6 \cdot (11 \cdot 6 - 6 \cdot 0) - 1 \cdot (6 \cdot 6 - 6 \cdot 0) + 0 = 6(66) - 1(36) = 396 - 36 = 360 > 0 \;\checkmark
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{All Hurwitz determinants positive} \Rightarrow \text{Stable system}}
$$

> **Verification:** The polynomial factors as $(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)$. All roots $\{-1, -2, -3\}$ are in LHP. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Domain | Input Required | Output | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Routh-Hurwitz** | s-domain | Characteristic polynomial | Number of RHP poles | Quick algebraic check; finding K-range |
| **Hurwitz Determinant** | s-domain | Polynomial coefficients | All LHP? (Yes/No) | Symbolic stability conditions |
| **Nyquist** | Frequency | $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ plot | Encirclements count | Systems with delays, conditional stability |
| **Bode** | Frequency | $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ log-magnitude | GM, PM | Design of compensators, relative stability |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 s-Plane Stability Regions

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["s-Plane Coordinate System"] --> B["Right Half Plane RHP"]
    A --> C["Imaginary Axis jω"]
    A --> D["Left Half Plane LHP"]
    B --> B1["UNSTABLE region: Re s greater than 0"]
    C --> C1["MARGINALLY STABLE: Re s equals 0"]
    D --> D1["STABLE region: Re s less than 0"]
    C1 --> C2["Pole on jω axis sustained oscillation"]
    D1 --> D2["Decaying exponential response"]
    B1 --> B2["Growing exponential response"]
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Feedback Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    R["R(s) Input"] --> S1["Summing Junction"]
    S1 --> G["G(s) Forward Path"]
    G --> Y["Y(s) Output"]
    Y --> F1["Feedback Path"]
    F1 --> H["H(s) Sensor"]
    H --> S2["Feedback Signal"]
    S2 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Routh-Hurwitz Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Start: Characteristic Polynomial"] --> B["Check all coefficients nonzero and same sign"]
    B --> C{"Necessary condition met?"}
    C -- No --> D["System Unstable or Special Case"]
    C -- Yes --> E["Construct Routh Array"]
    E --> F{"Any row of zeros?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Form Auxiliary Polynomial and Differentiate"]
    G --> E
    F -- No --> H["Examine First Column"]
    H --> I{"All same sign?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["System STABLE"]
    I -- No --> K["Count Sign Changes"]
    K --> L["Number of Sign Changes equals RHP Poles"]
    L --> M["System UNSTABLE"]
```

## 4.4 Nyquist Stability Analysis Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Open Loop Transfer Function GHs"] --> B["Substitute s equals j omega"]
    B --> C["Plot Nyquist Contour in s-plane"]
    C --> D["Map to GH j omega in complex plane"]
    D --> E["Count Clockwise Encirclements N of minus 1 j0"]
    E --> F["Determine Open Loop RHP Poles P"]
    F --> G["Apply N equals Z minus P"]
    G --> H{"Z equals 0?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Closed Loop STABLE"]
    H -- No --> J["Closed Loop UNSTABLE with Z RHP poles"]
```

## 4.5 Bode Plot Stability Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Plot Bode Magnitude and Phase of GH j omega"] --> B["Find omega_pc where phase equals minus 180"]
    A --> C["Find omega_gc where magnitude equals 0 dB"]
    B --> D["Read magnitude at omega_pc"]
    D --> E["GM dB equals negative of that magnitude"]
    C --> F["Read phase at omega_gc"]
    F --> G["PM equals 180 plus that phase"]
    E --> H{"GM greater than 0 dB and PM greater than 0 deg?"}
    G --> H
    H -- Yes --> I["STABLE with relative stability margins"]
    H -- No --> J["UNSTABLE or marginally stable"]
```

## 4.6 Comparative Analysis Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Algebraic["Algebraic Domain"]
        R["Routh-Hurwitz"]
        H2["Hurwitz Determinant"]
    end
    subgraph Frequency["Frequency Domain"]
        N["Nyquist Plot"]
        B["Bode Plot"]
    end
    R --> D1["Answers: How many RHP poles?"]
    H2 --> D2["Answers: Are all roots in LHP?"]
    N --> D3["Answers: How many encirclements?"]
    B --> D4["Answers: GM and PM for design"]
    D1 --> F["Common Goal: STABILITY VERIFICATION"]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define BIBO stability. What is the condition on the poles of a system for it to be BIBO stable?

**Model Answer:**
> BIBO (Bounded Input Bounded Output) stability means that for every bounded input signal, the output of the system remains bounded. The necessary and sufficient condition for an LTI system to be BIBO stable is that **all poles of the system transfer function must lie strictly in the left-half of the s-plane**, i.e., the real parts of all poles must be negative ($\Re(s_i) < 0$). If any pole lies on the $j\omega$-axis, the system is marginally stable; if any pole lies in the RHP, the system is unstable. **[3 Marks]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. What is the significance of the first column of the Routh array?

**Model Answer:**
> The Routh-Hurwitz criterion is an algebraic method to determine the number of poles of a characteristic polynomial in the right-half s-plane, without actually computing the roots. The significance of the first column of the Routh array is that **the number of sign changes in the first column equals the number of roots (poles) lying in the right-half s-plane**. For the system to be stable, all elements in the first column must have the **same sign** (typically all positive). **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to form the characteristic equation $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ before constructing the Routh array. **Always start with the CE.**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Sign errors in computing Routh array entries — re-derive $b_1$ formula carefully: $b_1 = (a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3)/a_1$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not mentioning the *necessary condition* (all coefficients real, non-zero, same sign) before constructing the array. This costs 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In Nyquist problems, students forget that encirclements are counted *clockwise* as positive. Always state the direction explicitly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

> **Q (A).** The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback system is:
> $$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s^2+4s+20)}$$
> **(a)** Determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Find the value of $K$ at which the system becomes marginally stable and determine the frequency of sustained oscillation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Form the characteristic equation.

$$
1 + G(s) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s^2+4s+20)} = 0
$$

$$
s(s+2)(s^2+4s+20) + K = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Expand the product.

$$
s(s+2) = s^2 + 2s
$$

$$
(s^2 + 2s)(s^2 + 4s + 20) = s^4 + 4s^3 + 20s^2 + 2s^3 + 8s^2 + 40s
$$

$$
= s^4 + 6s^3 + 28s^2 + 40s
$$

**Step 3:** Add $K$.

$$
\boxed{s^4 + 6s^3 + 28s^2 + 40s + K = 0}
$$

**Step 4:** Check necessary conditions.

All coefficients: $1, 6, 28, 40, K$ — non-zero and same sign (positive) only if $K > 0$.

**Step 5:** Construct Routh Array.

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^4 & 1 & 28 & K \\
s^3 & 6 & 40 & 0 \\
s^2 & b_1 & b_2 & 0 \\
s^1 & c_1 & 0 & 0 \\
s^0 & d_1 & 0 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute $b_1$ and $b_2$.

$$
b_1 = \frac{(6)(28) - (1)(40)}{6} = \frac{168 - 40}{6} = \frac{128}{6} = \frac{64}{3}
$$

$$
b_2 = \frac{(6)(K) - (1)(0)}{6} = K
$$

**Step 7:** Compute $c_1$.

$$
c_1 = \frac{b_1 \cdot 40 - 6 \cdot b_2}{b_1} = 40 - \frac{6K}{b_1} = 40 - \frac{6K \cdot 3}{64} = 40 - \frac{18K}{64} = 40 - \frac{9K}{32}
$$

**Step 8:** Compute $d_1$.

$$
d_1 = \frac{c_1 \cdot b_2 - b_1 \cdot 0}{c_1} = b_2 = K
$$

**Step 9:** Apply stability conditions (all first-column elements positive).

- $1 > 0$ ✓
- $6 > 0$ ✓
- $b_1 = 64/3 > 0$ ✓ (always positive)
- $c_1 = 40 - \frac{9K}{32} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K < \frac{40 \times 32}{9} = \frac{1280}{9} \approx 142.22$
- $d_1 = K > 0$

**Final Answer for (a):**

$$
\boxed{0 < K < \frac{1280}{9} \approx 142.22}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Marginal stability occurs when the first column has a zero entry.

Setting $c_1 = 0$:

$$
40 - \frac{9K}{32} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K = \frac{40 \times 32}{9} = \frac{1280}{9} \approx 142.22
$$

**Step 2:** To find the oscillation frequency, use the auxiliary polynomial from the $s^2$ row.

$$
A(s) = b_1 s^2 + b_2 = \frac{64}{3} s^2 + K
$$

At $K = 1280/9$:

$$
\frac{64}{3} s^2 + \frac{1280}{9} = 0
$$

$$
s^2 = -\frac{1280}{9} \times \frac{3}{64} = -\frac{1280 \times 3}{9 \times 64} = -\frac{3840}{576} = -\frac{20}{3}
$$

$$
s = \pm j\sqrt{\frac{20}{3}} = \pm j \cdot 2.582
$$

**Step 3:** Identify frequency of oscillation.

$$
\boxed{K_{marginal} = \frac{1280}{9} \approx 142.22, \quad \omega_{osc} = \sqrt{\frac{20}{3}} \approx 2.582 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Q (B).** A unity feedback control system has an open-loop transfer function:
> $$G(s) = \frac{K(s+4)}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$$
> **(a)** Using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, find the range of $K$ for closed-loop stability. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Comment on the type of stability and the value of $K$ for which the system is marginally stable. Find the corresponding frequency of oscillation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Form characteristic equation.

$$
1 + \frac{K(s+4)}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s(s+1)(s+2) + K(s+4) = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Expand.

$$
s(s+1)(s+2) = s(s^2 + 3s + 2) = s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s
$$

$$
s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + Ks + 4K = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{s^3 + 3s^2 + (2+K)s + 4K = 0}
$$

**Step 3:** Construct Routh Array.

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 2+K \\
s^2 & 3 & 4K \\
s^1 & b_1 & 0 \\
s^0 & c_1 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $b_1$.

$$
b_1 = \frac{3(2+K) - 1(4K)}{3} = \frac{6 + 3K - 4K}{3} = \frac{6 - K}{3}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $c_1$.

$$
c_1 = \frac{b_1 \cdot 4K - 3 \cdot 0}{b_1} = 4K
$$

**Step 6:** Apply stability conditions.

- $b_1 > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 6 - K > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K < 6$
- $c_1 > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K > 0$

**Final Answer for (a):**

$$
\boxed{0 < K < 6}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Marginal stability occurs at $b_1 = 0$:

$$
\frac{6 - K}{3} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K = 6
$$

**Step 2:** Auxiliary polynomial from $s^2$ row.

$$
A(s) = 3s^2 + 4K = 3s^2 + 24 = 0
$$

$$
s^2 = -8 \;\Rightarrow\; s = \pm j\sqrt{8} = \pm j \cdot 2\sqrt{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion.

$$
\boxed{K_{marginal} = 6, \quad \omega_{osc} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

At $K = 6$, the system exhibits **sustained oscillations** of frequency $2\sqrt{2}$ rad/s (marginally stable). For $0 < K < 6$, the system is **asymptotically stable**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ⚡**
>
> **1. Core Stability Condition:**
> - LTI stable ⟺ all poles in strict LHP ($\Re(s_i) < 0$)
> - Marginal: simple poles on $j\omega$-axis
> - Unstable: at least one pole in RHP or repeated $j\omega$-axis poles
>
> **2. Characteristic Equation:**
> - Unity feedback: $1 + G(s) = 0$
> - General feedback: $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$
>
> **3. Routh-Hurwitz Quick Steps:**
> - Verify all coefficients real, non-zero, same sign
> - Build array using $b_1 = (a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3)/a_1$
> - First column sign changes = number of RHP poles
>
> **4. Hurwitz Criterion:**
> - All leading principal minors $\Delta_i > 0$
> - Equivalent to Routh for low-order systems
>
> **5. Nyquist Criterion:**
> - $N = Z - P$
> - Stability: $Z = 0$ ⟹ $N = -P$ (anticlockwise = stable)
>
> **6. Bode Margins:**
> - $GM_{dB} = -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$
> - $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$
> - Design: $GM \geq 6$ dB, $PM \in [30°, 60°]$
>
> **7. Special Routh Cases:**
> - Zero in first column → use $\varepsilon \to 0^+$
> - Entire zero row → form auxiliary polynomial, differentiate
>
> **8. Common Exam Traps:**
> - Always form the CE first!
> - Don't confuse open-loop vs closed-loop stability
> - Hurwitz requires polynomial in **descending powers of $s$**
> - In Nyquist, encirclement direction must be stated explicitly
>
> **9. Real-World Connections:**
> - Aircraft autopilot → Nyquist/Bode
> - Power grid stabilizer → Routh-Hurwitz
> - Oscillator design → marginal stability intentional
> - Pacemaker/medical devices → strict BIBO stability
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two Port Network Parameters: Two Port Networks - Impedance, Admittance, Hybrid and Transmission Parameters - Conditions for Reciprocity and Symmetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEIT303 - NETWORK THEORY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Two Port Network Parameters: Two Port Networks - Impedance, Admittance, Hybrid and Transmission Parameters - Conditions for Reciprocity and Symmetry # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two-Port Network (Definition):** A *two-port network* is an electrical network that contains **two pairs of accessible terminals** (one pair designated as the **input port** and the other as the **output port**), enclosed within a closed boundary, such that the **net current entering each port equals the net current leaving it** and no external connections exist inside the network. A two-port network is completely characterized by **four real-time variables**: $V_1, I_1$ at Port 1 and $V_2, I_2$ at Port 2. Of these four variables, **any two may be chosen as independent** (excitations), and the remaining two are then treated as **dependent responses**. This selection of independent variables gives rise to **four fundamental parameter sets** of two-port analysis: | Independent Variables | Parameter Set | Full Name | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $I_1, I_2$ (currents) | **Z-parameters** | Impedance / Open-Circuit Parameters | | $V_1, V_2$ (voltages) | **Y-parameters** | Admittance / Short-Circuit Parameters | | $I_1, V_2$ (mixed) | **h-parameters** | Hybrid Parameters | | $V_2, I_2$ (output variables) | **T-parameters** | Transmission / ABCD Parameters | ## 1.2 Standard Reference Convention (KTU Convention) > [!NOTE] > At **Port 1** (input): $V_1$ is the voltage across the port and $I_1$ is the current **flowing into** the upper terminal. > At **Port 2** (output): $V_2$ is the voltage across the port and $I_2$ is the current **flowing out** of the upper terminal (i.e., $I_2$ flows *into* the network from the load side). The network is generally assumed to be **linear, passive, bilateral, and lumped** — the standard KTU pre-requisite assumption. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The House with Two Doors" Imagine a **black-box house** sitting between a **generator (source)** and a **load**. The house has exactly **two doors**: Door-1 is where the generator connects, Door-2 is where the load connects. We cannot see *what is inside* the house (its internal components: resistors, capacitors, inductors). We can only **measure** the four signals — voltage and current at each door. The **two-port parameters** are nothing but a set of *mathematical relationships* that allow us to **predict** what happens at one door when we *excite* the other door — **without ever opening the house**. - **Z-parameters** → we keep the doors' *currents* as inputs, ask: "What voltages appear?" - **Y-parameters** → we keep the doors' *voltages* as inputs, ask: "What currents appear?" - **h-parameters** → mixed strategy (one current, one voltage). - **ABCD parameters** → cascade-friendly: useful when **two such houses are connected in series** (cascaded). ## 1.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Two-port analysis is the **backbone of modern electronics design**: > - **Transistor amplifier design** (CE, CB, CC configurations use h-parameters directly). > - **Transmission line & cable modelling** (ABCD parameters describe cascaded line sections). > - **Filter design** (lattice, lattice-bridge, and crystal filters are described using Z or ABCD). > - **Microwave networks** (S-parameters — the high-frequency cousin of ABCD). > - **Control systems** (transfer functions are essentially a special form of two-port response). ## 1.5 The Significance of Reciprocity and Symmetry - **Reciprocity** answers: *"Does the network give the same response when input and output are swapped?"* — Equivalent to: *"Is the network bilateral?"* - **Symmetry** answers: *"Does the network look the same from both ports?"* — Equivalent to: *"Can I reverse the input/output labels and obtain the same network?"* Both properties are **deeply tied to internal topology** (presence/absence of dependent sources and series/parallel asymmetry). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Parameter Sets — Derivation of Governing Equations ### 2.1.1 Z-Parameters (Impedance / Open-Circuit Parameters) > [!IMPORTANT] > Independent variables are $I_1$ and $I_2$. The network equations are: $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= Z_{11}\,I_1 + Z_{12}\,I_2 \\ V_2 &= Z_{21}\,I_1 + Z_{22}\,I_2 \end{aligned} $$ In matrix form: $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = [Z]\,\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Meaning of each Z-parameter (measured under open-circuit conditions):** $$ Z_{11} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_1}\right|_{I_2=0}, \quad Z_{12} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_2}\right|_{I_1=0}, \quad Z_{21} = \left.\frac{V_2}{I_1}\right|_{I_2=0}, \quad Z_{22} = \left.\frac{V_2}{I_2}\right|_{I_1=0} $$ - $Z_{11}$ — **Driving-point impedance at Port 1** (output open). - $Z_{22}$ — **Driving-point impedance at Port 2** (input open). - $Z_{21}$ — **Forward transfer impedance** (output open). - $Z_{12}$ — **Reverse transfer impedance** (input open). ### 2.1.2 Y-Parameters (Admittance / Short-Circuit Parameters) > [!IMPORTANT] > Independent variables are $V_1$ and $V_2$. The network equations are: $$ \begin{aligned} I_1 &= Y_{11}\,V_1 + Y_{12}\,V_2 \\ I_2 &= Y_{21}\,V_1 + Y_{22}\,V_2 \end{aligned} $$ Matrix form: $$ \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Y_{11} & Y_{12} \\ Y_{21} & Y_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = [Y]\,\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Y-parameters are measured under short-circuit conditions:** $$ Y_{11} = \left.\frac{I_1}{V_1}\right|_{V_2=0}, \quad Y_{12} = \left.\frac{I_1}{V_2}\right|_{V_1=0}, \quad Y_{21} = \left.\frac{I_2}{V_1}\right|_{V_2=0}, \quad Y_{22} = \left.\frac{I_2}{V_2}\right\vert_{V_1=0} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Relationship:** Since $[Z][Y] = [I]$, the matrices are **inverses** of each other: > $$[Y] = [Z]^{-1}$$ ### 2.1.3 h-Parameters (Hybrid Parameters) > [!IMPORTANT] > Independent variables are $I_1$ and $V_2$. The network equations are: $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= h_{11}\,I_1 + h_{12}\,V_2 \\ I_2 &= h_{21}\,I_1 + h_{22}\,V_2 \end{aligned} $$ Matrix form: $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = [h]\,\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ **h-parameters — physical meanings:** $$ h_{11} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_1}\right\vert_{V_2=0} \;(\text{Input impedance, output short}), \quad h_{12} = \left.\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right\vert_{I_1=0} \;(\text{Reverse voltage ratio}) $$ $$ h_{21} = \left.\frac{I_2}{I_1}\right\vert_{V_2=0} \;(\text{Forward current gain}), \quad h_{22} = \left.\frac{I_2}{V_2}\right\vert_{I_1=0} \;(\text{Output admittance, input open}) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why "hybrid"?** Because the units are *mixed* — some are in $\Omega$, some in Siemens, some are dimensionless ratios. This is precisely the parameter set used in the **small-signal analysis of bipolar junction transistors (BJT)** in the CE configuration. ### 2.1.4 ABCD (Transmission) Parameters > [!IMPORTANT] > Independent variables are $V_2$ and $I_2$ (the *output* variables). The governing equations are: $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= A\,V_2 - B\,I_2 \\ I_1 &= C\,V_2 - D\,I_2 \end{aligned} $$ Matrix form (note the **negative sign on $-I_2$**): $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix} = [T]\,\begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ **ABCD parameters — physical meanings:** $$ A = \left.\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right\vert_{I_2=0} \;(\text{Reverse voltage ratio, open}), \quad B = -\left.\frac{V_1}{I_2}\right\vert_{V_2=0} \;(\text{Negative short-circuit transfer impedance}) $$ $$ C = \left.\frac{I_1}{V_2}\right\vert_{I_2=0} \;(\text{Reverse transfer admittance, open}), \quad D = -\left.\frac{I_1}{I_2}\right\vert_{V_2=0} \;(\text{Negative reverse current ratio, short}) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the negative sign on $I_2$?** ABCD parameters are used to cascade networks. When two networks are connected, the *output current* of the first network must equal the *input current* of the second. The convention $-I_2$ automatically aligns currents in cascade multiplication: $[T]_{\text{total}} = [T]_1 \cdot [T]_2$. ## 2.2 Conditions for Reciprocity and Symmetry > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reciprocity** = *The network is bilateral* (independent of port labelling). The **transfer parameter in the forward direction equals the transfer parameter in the reverse direction**. > **Symmetry** = *The network looks identical from both ports* (input and output are interchangeable). ### 2.2.1 Reciprocity Conditions | Parameter Set | Reciprocity Condition | Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Z** | $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ | Forward = reverse transfer impedance | | **Y** | $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ | Forward = reverse transfer admittance | | **h** | $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ | Sign correction (mixed units) | | **ABCD** | $\Delta T = AD - BC = 1$ | Determinant of $T$-matrix is unity | ### 2.2.2 Symmetry Conditions (a reciprocal network is *additionally* symmetric if…) | Parameter Set | Symmetry Condition | | :--- | :--- | | **Z** | $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ (and the network is reciprocal) | | **Y** | $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ (and the network is reciprocal) | | **h** | $h_{11}\,h_{22} - h_{12}\,h_{21} = 1$ (and the network is reciprocal) | | **ABCD** | $A = D$ (and $AD - BC = 1$) | > [!NOTE] > **Important Hierarchy:** A *symmetric* two-port is **always** reciprocal, but a *reciprocal* two-port is **not necessarily** symmetric. The symmetry condition is **stricter** than the reciprocity condition. ## 2.3 Complete KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet | Parameter | Governing Equations | Conditions (Reciprocal) | Conditions (Symmetric) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Z** | $V_1 = Z_{11}I_1 + Z_{12}I_2$
$V_2 = Z_{21}I_1 + Z_{22}I_2$ | $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ | $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ | | **Y** | $I_1 = Y_{11}V_1 + Y_{12}V_2$
$I_2 = Y_{21}V_1 + Y_{22}V_2$ | $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ | $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ | | **h** | $V_1 = h_{11}I_1 + h_{12}V_2$
$I_2 = h_{21}I_1 + h_{22}V_2$ | $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ | $h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21} = 1$ | | **ABCD** | $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$
$I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$ | $AD - BC = 1$ | $A = D$ | ## 2.4 Inter-Parameter Conversion Highlights (High-Yield for KTU Problems) > [!NOTE] > **Most frequently asked conversions (KTU 2024 trend):** $$ \begin{aligned} [Y] &= [Z]^{-1} \\ \Delta h &= h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21} \\ Z_{11} &= \frac{A}{C}, \quad Z_{12} = \frac{AD-BC}{C}, \quad Z_{21} = \frac{1}{C}, \quad Z_{22} = \frac{D}{C} \\ Y_{11} &= \frac{D}{B}, \quad Y_{12} = \frac{-(AD-BC)}{B}, \quad Y_{21} = \frac{-1}{B}, \quad Y_{22} = \frac{A}{B} \end{aligned} $$ **Cascade multiplication rule (only for ABCD):** $$ [T]_{\text{total}} = [T]_1 \cdot [T]_2 \cdot [T]_3 \cdots $$ ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility — Deeper Context - **Z-parameters** dominate at **low frequencies** when circuits can be opened safely. - **Y-parameters** dominate when circuits can be **shorted safely** (e.g., high-frequency VLSI where shorting is easier than opening). - **h-parameters** are the **workhorse of BJT small-signal modelling** at audio frequencies. - **ABCD parameters** dominate **transmission line modelling, cascaded filter design, and microwave networks** — anywhere "in-line" connections are made. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > This section provides the **exhaustive mathematical derivations** that KTU examiners love to test. Every algebraic transition is shown step-by-step. ## 3.1 Derivation: $[Y] = [Z]^{-1}$ (Why the Two Matrices are Inverses) **Starting point** — the Z-parameter equations: $$ V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2 \quad \text{(Eq. 1)} $$ $$ V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2 \quad \text{(Eq. 2)} $$ **Step 1 — Express as a matrix equation:** $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \Rightarrow \quad [V] = [Z][I] $$ **Step 2 — Pre-multiply both sides by $[Z]^{-1}$:** $$ [Z]^{-1}[V] = [Z]^{-1}[Z][I] = [I] \quad \Rightarrow \quad [I] = [Z]^{-1}[V] $$ **Step 3 — Identify $[Z]^{-1}$ as $[Y]$:** By comparing with the standard Y-equation $[I] = [Y][V]$, we conclude: $$ [Y] = [Z]^{-1} = \frac{1}{Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}} \begin{bmatrix} Z_{22} & -Z_{12} \\ -Z_{21} & Z_{11} \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4 — Element-wise expansion:** $$ \begin{aligned} Y_{11} &= \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z} \quad & Y_{12} &= \frac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z} \quad & \text{where } \Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21} \\ Y_{21} &= \frac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z} \quad & Y_{22} &= \frac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** Short-circuiting a port (the $Y$ approach) is the **dual** of opening a port (the $Z$ approach). The matrix inverse relation mathematically captures this duality. ## 3.2 Derivation: Reciprocity Condition for h-Parameters **Claim:** A two-port is reciprocal in h-parameters **if and only if** $h_{12} = -h_{21}$. **Starting point** — h-parameter equations: $$ V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2 \quad \text{(Eq. 3)} $$ $$ I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2 \quad \text{(Eq. 4)} $$ **Step 1 — Convert to Z-parameters** by eliminating $V_1, V_2$ and solving for $V_1, V_2$ in terms of $I_1, I_2$. From Eq. 4: $$ I_2 - h_{22} V_2 = h_{21} I_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2 = \frac{I_2 - h_{21} I_1}{h_{22}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute into Eq. 3:** $$ V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} \cdot \frac{I_2 - h_{21} I_1}{h_{22}} $$ $$ V_1 = \left(h_{11} - \frac{h_{12} h_{21}}{h_{22}}\right) I_1 + \frac{h_{12}}{h_{22}} I_2 $$ **Step 3 — Apply reciprocity in Z-domain** ($Z_{12} = Z_{21}$). The $Z$-matrix from h-parameters is: $$ [Z] = \frac{1}{h_{22}} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta h & h_{12} \\ -h_{21} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} $$ **Step 4 — Set $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$:** $$ \frac{h_{12}}{h_{22}} = \frac{-h_{21}}{h_{22}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_{12} = -h_{21} $$ > [!NOTE] > The negative sign appears **only in h-parameters** because the choice of mixed independent variables ($I_1$ and $V_2$) creates a sign convention mismatch that does not exist in pure-current (Z) or pure-voltage (Y) parameter sets. ## 3.3 Derivation: Reciprocity Condition for ABCD Parameters ($AD - BC = 1$) **Claim:** A two-port is reciprocal in ABCD parameters **if and only if** $\det[T] = AD - BC = 1$. **Step 1 — Express V₁, I₁ in terms of V₂, I₂:** $$ V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2 \quad \text{(Eq. 5)} $$ $$ I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2 \quad \text{(Eq. 6)} $$ **Step 2 — Swap input and output (Port 1 ↔ Port 2)**. After swapping, the *new* $V_1', I_1'$ at the *original* port 2 are functions of the *new* $V_2', I_2'$ at the *original* port 1: $$ V_2' = A V_1' - B I_1' \quad \text{(with the same A, B, C, D due to reciprocity)} $$ $$ I_2' = C V_1' - D I_1' $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $V_1', I_1'$ in terms of $V_2', I_2'$** using Cramer's rule: $$ V_1' = \frac{D V_2' - B I_2'}{AD - BC}, \quad I_1' = \frac{-C V_2' + A I_2'}{AD - BC} $$ **Step 4 — Reciprocity demands** that the *forward* and *reverse* relations look identical, which requires the matrix to behave like a pure transformer (determinant = 1): $$ \boxed{AD - BC = 1} $$ **Sanity check using a simple lossless line** (purely resistive $R$): $A=1, B=R, C=0, D=1 \Rightarrow AD-BC = 1 \cdot 1 - R \cdot 0 = 1$. ✓ ## 3.4 Derivation: Symmetry Conditions from Reciprocity > [!NOTE] > **Symmetry = Reciprocity + Equality of driving-point parameters.** A **symmetric** network is one in which the **driving-point impedance (or admittance) at Port 1 equals that at Port 2**, given reciprocity. Thus: - **Z symmetric:** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ (and $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$). - **Y symmetric:** $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ (and $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$). - **ABCD symmetric:** $A = D$ (and $AD - BC = 1$). - **h symmetric:** $\det[h] = h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21} = 1$ (and $h_{12} = -h_{21}$). **Derivation of h-symmetry condition:** From Eq. (3.2) above, the Z-matrix obtained is: $$ [Z] = \frac{1}{h_{22}} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta h & h_{12} \\ -h_{21} & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ For symmetry: $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$: $$ \frac{\Delta h}{h_{22}} = \frac{1}{h_{22}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta h = 1 $$ Combined with the reciprocity condition $h_{12} = -h_{21}$: $$ \boxed{h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = 1} $$ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — T-Network Z-Parameters > [!NOTE] > **Reference circuit (T-network):** Series $Z_1 = 5\,\Omega$ (left arm), shunt $Z_3 = 5\,\Omega$ (middle vertical arm), series $Z_2 = 10\,\Omega$ (right arm). Find Z-parameters and check reciprocity & symmetry. **Step 1 — Apply $I_2 = 0$ (output open).** With Port 2 open, the current $I_1$ flows through $Z_1$ and $Z_3$ in series: $$ Z_{11} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_1}\right\vert_{I_2=0} = Z_1 + Z_3 = 5 + 5 = 10\,\Omega $$ $$ V_2 = I_1 \cdot Z_3 = 5 I_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_{21} = \left.\frac{V_2}{I_1}\right\vert_{I_2=0} = Z_3 = 5\,\Omega $$ **Step 2 — Apply $I_1 = 0$ (input open).** With Port 1 open, the current $I_2$ flows through $Z_2$ and $Z_3$ in series: $$ Z_{22} = \left.\frac{V_2}{I_2}\right\vert_{I_1=0} = Z_2 + Z_3 = 10 + 5 = 15\,\Omega $$ $$ V_1 = I_2 \cdot Z_3 = 5 I_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_{12} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_2}\right\vert_{I_1=0} = Z_3 = 5\,\Omega $$ **Step 3 — Resulting Z-matrix:** $$ [Z] = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 5 \\ 5 & 15 \end{bmatrix}\,\Omega $$ **Step 4 — Reciprocity check:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 5\,\Omega$ ✓ → **Network is reciprocal.** **Step 5 — Symmetry check:** $Z_{11} = 10\,\Omega \ne Z_{22} = 15\,\Omega$ → **Network is NOT symmetric.** ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — T-Network ABCD Parameters (Same Network) **Step 1 — To find $A$ and $C$, open-circuit Port 2 ($I_2 = 0$):** With $I_2 = 0$, no current flows in $Z_2$, so the network is just a voltage divider formed by $Z_1$ and $Z_3$: $$ V_1 = (Z_1 + Z_3) I_1, \quad V_2 = Z_3 \cdot I_1 $$ $$ A = \left.\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right\vert_{I_2=0} = \frac{Z_1 + Z_3}{Z_3} = \frac{5+5}{5} = 2 $$ $$ C = \left.\frac{I_1}{V_2}\right\vert_{I_2=0} = \frac{1}{Z_3} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2\,\text{S} $$ **Step 2 — To find $B$ and $D$, short-circuit Port 2 ($V_2 = 0$):** With $V_2 = 0$, $Z_2$ and $Z_3$ become parallel: $$ Z_2 \parallel Z_3 = \frac{10 \cdot 5}{10 + 5} = \frac{50}{15} = \frac{10}{3}\,\Omega $$ $$ I_1 = \frac{V_1}{Z_1 + (Z_2 \parallel Z_3)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = I_1 \left(5 + \frac{10}{3}\right) = I_1 \cdot \frac{25}{3} $$ The current $I_2$ entering the short is found by current divider: the fraction of $I_1$ that flows through $Z_3$ (which equals $-I_2$, since $I_2$ leaves the network from the upper terminal of Port 2): $$ -I_2 = I_1 \cdot \frac{Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_3} = I_1 \cdot \frac{10}{15} = \frac{2 I_1}{3} $$ $$ D = -\left.\frac{I_1}{I_2}\right\vert_{V_2=0} = -\frac{I_1}{-2I_1/3} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 $$ $$ B = -\left.\frac{V_1}{I_2}\right\vert_{V_2=0} = -\frac{I_1 \cdot 25/3}{-2 I_1/3} = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5\,\Omega $$ **Step 3 — Resulting ABCD matrix:** $$ [T] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 12.5 \\ 0.2 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4 — Reciprocity check:** $AD - BC = 2 \cdot 1.5 - 12.5 \cdot 0.2 = 3 - 2.5 = 0.5 \ne 1$ — Wait, let me recheck. > [!NOTE] > Recheck: The T-network ABCD formula (standard) is: > $$A = 1 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_3}, \quad B = Z_1 + Z_2 + \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3}, \quad C = \frac{1}{Z_3}, \quad D = 1 + \frac{Z_2}{Z_3}$$ Substituting: $A = 1 + 1 = 2$, $B = 5 + 10 + 10 = 25$, $C = 0.2$, $D = 1 + 2 = 3$. $$ \det[T] = AD - BC = 2(3) - 25(0.2) = 6 - 5 = 1 \quad \checkmark $$ (The earlier 12.5 was an error; the correct $B$ for this T-network is $25\,\Omega$.) **Step 5 — Symmetry check:** $A = 2 \ne D = 3$ → **NOT symmetric** (consistent with Z-domain conclusion). > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Insight:** The reciprocity condition $\det[T] = 1$ must hold for any *passive* (lossless or lossy) bilateral network. If a student's answer violates this, either the network is **non-reciprocal** (contains dependent sources) or a calculation error exists. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Generic Two-Port Network Structure ```mermaid graph LR nodeSrc["Source

T_total = T1 × T2
(Matrix Multiplication)"] -.Annotation.-> nodeN2A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Level Functional Architecture Insight:** Among the four parameter sets, **only ABCD permits simple matrix multiplication for cascaded networks**. For Z or Y parameters, the cascade combination requires a more involved *star-mesh* or *chain* transformation. This is why ABCD is the *de facto* standard for **transmission line** and **cascaded filter** design in KTU problems. ## 4.5 Quick-Reference Visual Map: Reciprocity vs Symmetry ```mermaid graph TD nodeStart["Two-Port Network"] --> nodeQ1{"Is Z12 = Z21?"} nodeQ1 -- "YES" --> nodeRec["Network is RECIPROCAL"] nodeQ1 -- "NO" --> nodeNonRec["Network is NON-RECIPROCAL
(Contains controlled sources)"] nodeRec --> nodeQ2{"Is Z11 = Z22 also?"} nodeQ2 -- "YES" --> nodeSym["Network is SYMMETRIC
(Reciprocal + Symmetric)"] nodeQ2 -- "NO" --> nodeRecOnly["Network is RECIPROCAL
but NOT symmetric"] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]** > **Q1.** Define a two-port network. List the four parameter sets used to characterize a linear, bilateral, passive two-port network, specifying the independent variable in each case. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A two-port network is an electrical network enclosed within a closed boundary having two pairs of accessible terminals — one designated as the **input port** (Port 1) and the other as the **output port** (Port 2) — with the constraint that the net current entering one terminal of a port equals the net current leaving the other terminal of the same port. The four parameter sets are: | Parameter Set | Independent Variables | | :--- | :--- | | **Z-parameters** | $I_1$ and $I_2$ (port currents) | | **Y-parameters** | $V_1$ and $V_2$ (port voltages) | | **h-parameters** | $I_1$ and $V_2$ (one current, one voltage) | | **ABCD parameters** | $V_2$ and $I_2$ (output port variables) | --- > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]** > **Q2.** State the conditions for **reciprocity** and **symmetry** of a two-port network in terms of (i) Z-parameters and (ii) ABCD parameters. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **For Z-parameters:** - **Reciprocity:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ - **Symmetry:** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ (in addition to reciprocity) **For ABCD parameters:** - **Reciprocity:** $AD - BC = 1$ - **Symmetry:** $A = D$ (in addition to $AD - BC = 1$) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 4 Choice 1]** > > **QUESTION A (14 Marks):** > For the T-network shown below, find the **(a)** Z-parameters, and **(b)** verify the conditions for reciprocity and symmetry. > > *(Network: $Z_1 = 5\,\Omega$ in series, $Z_3 = 5\,\Omega$ shunt, $Z_2 = 10\,\Omega$ in series.)* ### 5.2.A — Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Z-parameter Determination (7 marks) **Step 1 — Identify the network topology.** The given network is a T-network: $Z_1$ in the input series arm, $Z_3$ in the shunt (middle) arm, $Z_2$ in the output series arm. **Step 2 — Compute $Z_{11}$ and $Z_{21}$ by opening Port 2 ($I_2 = 0$):** With $I_2 = 0$, the entire input current $I_1$ flows through the series combination $Z_1 + Z_3$. $$ V_1 = I_1 (Z_1 + Z_3) = I_1 (5 + 5) = 10\,I_1 $$ $$ Z_{11} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_1}\right\vert_{I_2=0} = \frac{10\,I_1}{I_1} = 10\,\Omega $$ The voltage at Port 2 is the voltage across $Z_3$ alone (no current in $Z_2$): $$ V_2 = I_1 \cdot Z_3 = 5 I_1 $$ $$ Z_{21} = \left.\frac{V_2}{I_1}\right\vert_{I_2=0} = \frac{5 I_1}{I_1} = 5\,\Omega $$ **Step 3 — Compute $Z_{12}$ and $Z_{22}$ by opening Port 1 ($I_1 = 0$):** With $I_1 = 0$, the current $I_2$ flows through $Z_2$ and $Z_3$ in series. $$ V_2 = I_2 (Z_2 + Z_3) = I_2 (10 + 5) = 15\,I_2 $$ $$ Z_{22} = \left.\frac{V_2}{I_2}\right\vert_{I_1=0} = \frac{15\,I_2}{I_2} = 15\,\Omega $$ The voltage at Port 1 is the voltage across $Z_3$ alone (no current in $Z_1$): $$ V_1 = I_2 \cdot Z_3 = 5 I_2 $$ $$ Z_{12} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_2}\right\vert_{I_1=0} = \frac{5 I_2}{I_2} = 5\,\Omega $$ **Step 4 — Z-matrix:** $$ \boxed{[Z] = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 5 \\ 5 & 15 \end{bmatrix}\,\Omega} $$ #### Part (b) — Reciprocity and Symmetry Verification (7 marks) **Step 1 — Reciprocity check:** Compare off-diagonal elements: $Z_{12} = 5\,\Omega$ and $Z_{21} = 5\,\Omega$. $$ Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 5\,\Omega \quad \checkmark $$ **Conclusion:** The network **is reciprocal** (bilateral). **Step 2 — Symmetry check:** Compare diagonal elements: $Z_{11} = 10\,\Omega$ and $Z_{22} = 15\,\Omega$. $$ Z_{11} = 10\,\Omega \ne 15\,\Omega = Z_{22} $$ **Conclusion:** The network is **NOT symmetric** (the two ports are *not* electrically interchangeable — because $Z_1 \ne Z_2$). **Step 3 — Final summary table:** | Condition | Test | Result | Status | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Reciprocity | $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$? | $5 = 5$ | **Satisfied** | | Symmetry | $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$? | $10 \ne 15$ | **Not satisfied** | --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 4 Choice 2]** > > **QUESTION B (14 Marks):** > For the T-network with $Z_1 = 5\,\Omega, Z_2 = 10\,\Omega, Z_3 = 5\,\Omega$, determine the **(a)** ABCD (transmission) parameters, and **(b)** the Y-parameters using the conversion formulas. State whether the network is reciprocal and symmetric in ABCD domain. ### 5.2.B — Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — ABCD Parameter Determination (7 marks) **Step 1 — Recall the standard T-network ABCD formulas:** $$ A = 1 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_3}, \quad B = Z_1 + Z_2 + \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3}, \quad C = \frac{1}{Z_3}, \quad D = 1 + \frac{Z_2}{Z_3} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** For $A$: $$ A = 1 + \frac{5}{5} = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ For $B$: $$ B = 5 + 10 + \frac{5 \cdot 10}{5} = 15 + 10 = 25\,\Omega $$ For $C$: $$ C = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2\,\text{S} $$ For $D$: $$ D = 1 + \frac{10}{5} = 1 + 2 = 3 $$ **Step 3 — ABCD matrix:** $$ \boxed{[T] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 25 \\ 0.2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}} $$ **Step 4 — Reciprocity and symmetry check:** Reciprocity: $$ \det[T] = AD - BC = 2(3) - 25(0.2) = 6 - 5 = 1 \quad \checkmark $$ Symmetry: $$ A = 2 \ne 3 = D \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Not symmetric.} $$ **Conclusion:** The network is **reciprocal but not symmetric** in the ABCD domain — consistent with the Z-domain analysis. #### Part (b) — Y-Parameter Determination (7 marks) **Step 1 — Apply the standard conversion formulas from ABCD to Y:** $$ Y_{11} = \frac{D}{B}, \quad Y_{12} = \frac{-(AD-BC)}{B}, \quad Y_{21} = \frac{-1}{B}, \quad Y_{22} = \frac{A}{B} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** For $Y_{11}$: $$ Y_{11} = \frac{D}{B} = \frac{3}{25} = 0.12\,\text{S} $$ For $Y_{12}$: $$ Y_{12} = \frac{-(AD-BC)}{B} = \frac{-1}{25} = -0.04\,\text{S} $$ For $Y_{21}$: $$ Y_{21} = \frac{-1}{B} = \frac{-1}{25} = -0.04\,\text{S} $$ For $Y_{22}$: $$ Y_{22} = \frac{A}{B} = \frac{2}{25} = 0.08\,\text{S} $$ **Step 3 — Y-matrix:** $$ \boxed{[Y] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.12 & -0.04 \\ -0.04 & 0.08 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{S}} $$ **Step 4 — Reciprocity and symmetry check:** Reciprocity: $Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -0.04\,\text{S}$ ✓ → **Reciprocal.** Symmetry: $Y_{11} = 0.12 \ne 0.08 = Y_{22}$ → **Not symmetric.** **Step 5 — Verification using $[Y] = [Z]^{-1}$:** From earlier, $[Z] = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 5 \\ 5 & 15 \end{bmatrix}$. $$ \det[Z] = 10 \cdot 15 - 5 \cdot 5 = 150 - 25 = 125 $$ $$ [Z]^{-1} = \frac{1}{125}\begin{bmatrix} 15 & -5 \\ -5 & 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.12 & -0.04 \\ -0.04 & 0.08 \end{bmatrix} \quad \checkmark $$ **Conclusion:** The matrix conversion is **internally consistent** across Z, Y, and ABCD domains. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls & Mark Loss Hotspots** > > 1. **Sign convention error in ABCD:** $I_2$ is defined as the current *leaving* the network's upper Port 2 terminal. The matrix equation uses **$-I_2$**. Many students incorrectly use $+I_2$, leading to wrong signs for $B$ and $D$ — losing **2 to 3 marks** in a 14-mark question. > 2. **Forgetting the determinant condition:** In ABCD reciprocity, students often write $AD = BC$ instead of $AD - BC = 1$. The correct form is $AD - BC = 1$. > 3. **Hybrid-parameter sign:** Reciprocity in h-parameters is $h_{12} = -h_{21}$, **not** $h_{12} = h_{21}$ as in Z and Y. This sign difference is a classic KTU trap. > 4. **Cascade confusion:** Cascade multiplication is valid **only for ABCD** parameters. For Z and Y, the *chain* matrix must be used, or one must convert to ABCD first. > 5. **Unit mismatches:** $Z$ in $\Omega$, $Y$ in S, $h$ has mixed units. The $h_{11}\,h_{22} - h_{12}\,h_{21}$ expression is **dimensionless** — students should verify the units cancel before claiming $\Delta h = 1$. > 6. **Symmetry vs Reciprocity confusion:** A symmetric network is **always** reciprocal, but a reciprocal network is **not always** symmetric. Students often claim "symmetric" after only verifying reciprocity — losing marks for the missing equality check. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Quick-Reference Card for KTU 2024 — Two-Port Networks** ### Core Concepts to Memorize - ☐ A two-port has **two ports**, **four variables** ($V_1, I_1, V_2, I_2$), and **two independent** variables. - ☐ The four parameter sets are **Z, Y, h, ABCD** — each with its own choice of independent variables. - ☐ Z-parameters are measured with the **opposite port open**; Y-parameters with the **opposite port shorted**; h-parameters use **mixed test conditions**; ABCD uses **cascade output variables** with a **negative sign** on $I_2$. ### High-Yield Formula Triads - ☐ **$[Y] = [Z]^{-1}$** (Inverse duality). - ☐ **Reciprocity in Z:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ — **in Y:** $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ — **in h:** $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ — **in ABCD:** $AD - BC = 1$. - ☐ **Symmetry adds:** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ / $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ / $A = D$ / $\Delta h = 1$. - ☐ **ABCD cascade:** $[T]_{\text{total}} = [T]_1 \cdot [T]_2 \cdot [T]_3 \cdots$ ### Conversion Formulas (Most Frequently Asked) - ☐ $Z_{11} = A/C$, $Z_{12} = (AD-BC)/C$, $Z_{21} = 1/C$, $Z_{22} = D/C$. - ☐ $Y_{11} = D/B$, $Y_{12} = -(AD-BC)/B$, $Y_{21} = -1/B$, $Y_{22} = A/B$. ### Application Domains (KTU Theory & Viva) - ☐ **Z-parameters** → Low-frequency, BJT, FET (T-model). - ☐ **Y-parameters** → VLSI, high-frequency short-circuit friendly. - ☐ **h-parameters** → BJT CE-configuration small-signal. - ☐ **ABCD parameters** → Transmission lines, cascaded filters, microwave. ### Common Exam-Style Numerical Approach 1. ☐ Identify network topology (T, π, L, lattice). 2. ☐ Choose parameter set per problem statement. 3. ☐ Apply correct test conditions (open for Z, short for Y). 4. ☐ Compute all four parameters systematically. 5. ☐ Verify reciprocity and symmetry using the appropriate condition. 6. ☐ If conversions are required, use the standard conversion table. ### Sign Convention Reminder - ☐ $I_1$ flows **into** Port 1 (upper terminal). - ☐ $I_2$ flows **out of** Port 2 (upper terminal, towards load). - ☐ ABCD matrix uses $-I_2$ to enable cascade multiplication. ### Common Network Shortcuts (Must-Memorize) - ☐ **T-network Z-parameters:** $Z_{11} = Z_1 + Z_3$, $Z_{22} = Z_2 + Z_3$, $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = Z_3$. - ☐ **π-network Y-parameters:** $Y_{11} = Y_A + Y_C$, $Y_{22} = Y_B + Y_C$, $Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -Y_C$. - ☐ **T-network ABCD:** $A = 1 + Z_1/Z_3$, $B = Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_1 Z_2/Z_3$, $C = 1/Z_3$, $D = 1 + Z_2/Z_3$. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Tip:** Always re-check that your parameter matrix passes reciprocity (e.g., $AD - BC = 1$ for ABCD). A numerical mismatch in this determinant is the **fastest single-step self-check** during a 14-mark numerical problem — it instantly reveals a calculation error in $A, B, C,$ or $D$.

topics.title: Interrelationships of Different Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEIT303 - NETWORK THEORY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Interrelationships of Different Parameters # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In **Two-Port Network Analysis**, a linear time-invariant (LTI) bilateral two-port network can be completely characterized by any **two of the four port variables** (V₁, V₂, I₁, I₂). Since there are 4 variables and 2 independent equations are required, we obtain **C(4,2) = 6 possible parameter sets**: | # | Parameter Set | Independent Variables | Dependent Variables | Matrix Form | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Z-parameters** (Open-circuit Impedance) | I₁, I₂ | V₁, V₂ | [V] = [Z][I] | | 2 | **Y-parameters** (Short-circuit Admittance) | V₁, V₂ | I₁, I₂ | [I] = [Y][V] | | 3 | **h-parameters** (Hybrid / Series-Parallel) | I₁, V₂ | V₁, I₂ | Mixed | | 4 | **g-parameters** (Inverse Hybrid) | V₁, I₂ | I₁, V₂ | Mixed | | 5 | **ABCD-parameters** (Transmission / Chain) | V₂, I₂ | V₁, I₁ | Cascade | | 6 | **A'B'C'D'-parameters** (Inverse Transmission) | V₁, I₁ | V₂, I₂ | Reverse Cascade | The **"Interrelationships of Different Parameters"** refers to the systematic mathematical procedures that allow conversion of one set of two-port parameters into any other set, given that the network is linear and bilateral. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must master conversion of **Z ↔ Y, Z ↔ h, Z ↔ ABCD, Y ↔ h, Y ↔ ABCD, h ↔ g**, and **ABCD ↔ A'B'C'D'**. These conversions are frequently tested in Part B (14-mark) questions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Translator" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: International Currency Exchange** > > Imagine you are an international trader. The price of a commodity can be quoted in **six different currencies** — USD, EUR, GBP, JPY, INR, AUD. The commodity is the same; only the **language (units) of expression** differs. To do business, you must know the **exchange-rate formulas** that convert one currency to another. > > Similarly, the **physical two-port network** is the "commodity". The Z, Y, h, g, ABCD, and A'B'C'D' parameters are the "six currencies" in which the network's external behavior is described. The **interconversion formulas** are the "exchange rates" — they allow an engineer who has measured one set of parameters to predict the behavior of the network in any other configuration (e.g., cascaded, parallel, or feedback). ## 1.3 Why Interrelationships Matter - **Design Flexibility:** A transistor is naturally characterized by **h-parameters** at high frequencies by **Y-parameters**, at low frequencies by **Z-parameters**, and in a cascade by **ABCD-parameters**. A unified design requires all. - **Cascade Analysis:** ABCD matrices multiply directly; h, g do not. So engineers convert h→ABCD for cascading. - **Measurement Constraints:** Z-parameters need open-circuit tests (impractical at RF); Y-parameters need short-circuit tests. Conversion enables computation from available data. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics used in this Module:** > - **ΔZ** = det[Z] = Z₁₁Z₂₂ − Z₁₂Z₂₁ (in **Ω²**) > - **ΔY** = det[Y] = Y₁₁Y₂₂ − Y₁₂Y₂₁ (in **S²** or **mho²**) > - **Δh** = det[h] = h₁₁h₂₂ − h₁₂h₂₁ (dimensionless for h₂₂, **Ω** for h₁₁) > - **Δg** = det[g] = g₁₁g₂₂ − g₁₂g₂₁ > - **AD − BC = 1** (fundamental property of ABCD parameters for any network) > - **A'D' − B'C' = 1** (fundamental property of inverse transmission parameters) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Two-Port Definitions For a two-port network: $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{[Z-parameters]} $$ $$ \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Y_{11} & Y_{12} \\ Y_{21} & Y_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{[Y-parameters]} $$ $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{[h-parameters]} $$ $$ \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} g_{11} & g_{12} \\ g_{21} & g_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{[g-parameters]} $$ $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{[ABCD-parameters]} $$ $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A' & B' \\ C' & D' \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ -I_1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{[A'B'C'D'-parameters]} $$ ## 2.2 Why and How the Interrelationships Work The **Why:** All six parameter sets describe the **same physical network**. Therefore, the equations in one form must be algebraically equivalent to those in any other form. The interconversion process simply expresses the equations of one set in terms of the variables and coefficients of the other set. The **How (Procedure):** 1. Start with the defining equations of the source parameter set. 2. Apply algebraic manipulation (substitution, Cramer's rule, matrix inversion) to express the dependent variables of the target parameter set in terms of its independent variables. 3. Identify the coefficients of the new independent variables as the target parameters. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Interconversion Master Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Conversion Table — Memorize the highlighted ones for KTU exams.** All Δ symbols refer to determinants of the **source** matrix unless otherwise stated. | Target →
Source ↓ | **Z-parameters** | **Y-parameters** | **h-parameters** | **g-parameters** | **ABCD-parameters** | **A'B'C'D'-parameters** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Z** | — | Z₂₂/ΔZ
−Z₁₂/ΔZ
−Z₂₁/ΔZ
Z₁₁/ΔZ | ΔZ/Z₂₂
Z₁₂/Z₂₂
−Z₂₁/Z₂₂
1/Z₂₂ | 1/Z₁₁
−Z₁₂/Z₁₁
Z₂₁/Z₁₁
ΔZ/Z₁₁ | Z₁₁/Z₂₁
ΔZ/Z₂₁
1/Z₂₁
Z₂₂/Z₂₁ | Z₂₂/Z₁₂
ΔZ/Z₁₂

Z₁₁/Z₁₂ | | **Y** | Y₂₂/ΔY
−Y₁₂/ΔY

Y₁₁/ΔY | — | 1/Y₁₁

Y₂₁/Y₁₁

Y₁₂/Y₂₂

−1/Y₁₂
−ΔY/Y₁₂
−Y₂₂/Y₁₂ | | **h** | Δh/h₂₂
h₁₂/h₂₂
−h₂₁/h₂₂
1/h₂₂ | 1/h₁₁
−h₁₂/h₁₁
h₂₁/h₁₁
Δh/h₁₁ | — | h₁₁/Δh
h₁₂/Δh
−h₂₁/Δh
h₂₂/Δh | −Δh/h₂₁
−h₁₁/h₂₁
−h₂₂/h₂₁
−1/h₂₁ | — | | **g** | 1/g₁₁
−g₁₂/g₁₁
g₂₁/g₁₁
Δg/g₁₁ | Δg/g₂₂
g₁₂/g₂₂
−g₂₁/g₂₂
1/g₂₂ | g₂₂/Δg
−g₁₂/Δg
−g₂₁/Δg
g₁₁/Δg | — | — | — | | **ABCD** | A/C
Δ/C
1/C
D/C | D/B
−Δ/B
−1/B
A/B | B/D
Δ/D
−1/D
C/D | C/A
−Δ/A

B/A | — | D/Δ
B/Δ

A/Δ | | **A'B'C'D'** | A'/C'
Δ'/C'
1/C'
D'/C' | D'/B'
−Δ'/B'
−1/B'
A'/B' | B'/A'
−Δ'/A'

C'/A' | C'/D'

−1/D'

C'/Δ'
